The discourse is directed toward the idol-worshippers among the polytheists, even if it might include others like the People of the Book according to the breadth of its meaning. This is due to the use of the relative pronoun mā (ما) and the fact that the Surah is Meccan, from among the earliest parts of the Quran revealed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

The idol-worshippers used to worship idols to draw near through their worship to their lords (أربابها), and through those lords to the Lord of lords, who is Allah, the Glorified. They would say: "We, with the material human impurities and the filth of sins and offenses upon us, have no path to the Lord of lords due to the purity and sanctity of His presence; there is no proportion between us and Him.

Therefore, it is necessary that we draw near to Him through the most beloved of His creatures to Him, who are the lords of the idols to whom Allah has delegated the matter of managing His creation. We draw near to them through their idols and statues. We only worship the idols so that they may be intercessors (شفعاء) for us with Allah to bring us good and repel from us evil." Thus, the worship (العبادة) — the act of ultimate submission and devotion — actually falls upon the idols, while the intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — belongs to their lords, though it is sometimes attributed to the idols themselves.

In the discourse, the phrase ما لا يَضُرُّهُمْ وَلا يَنْفَعُهُمْ (that which neither harms them nor benefits them) has been placed in the position of the idols to point to the location of their error in their claim. This is because this endeavor would only succeed for them if these idols were harmful or beneficial in affairs and were entities possessing consciousness (ذوات شعور) of the worship and the drawing near, such that they would be pleased with their worshippers for their worship of them and thus intercede—or their lords would intercede for them—with Allah, if Allah approved of their intercession. Yet these are dead bodies that perceive nothing and neither harm nor benefit anything.

Allah, the Glorified, commanded His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to argue against the invalidity of their claim of intercession—in addition to what is signaled by His saying, "neither harms them nor benefits them"—with His saying: قُلْ أَتُنَبِّئُونَ اللهَ بِما لا يَعْلَمُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَلا فِي الْأَرْضِ (Say, ‘Do you inform Allah of something He does not know in the heavens or in the earth?’). The gist of it is that Allah, the Glorified, has no knowledge of these intercessors in any part of the heavens or the earth. Thus, this claim of yours is your informing Him of what He does not know, which is among the ugliest of fabrications and the most heinous of obstinacy. How can there be anything in existence that Allah does not know, while He knows what is in the heavens and the earth?

The question is rhetorical, and the negation of knowledge of the existence of intercessors is a metaphor (كناية) for the negation of their existence. Perhaps this expression was chosen because intercession is something constituted by knowledge in its very essence; for intercession only occurs if the one before whom intercession is made is aware of the existence of the intercessor and his intercession. If it is assumed that He does not know of the intercessors, how can intercession be realized before Him while He does not know?

His saying, سُبْحانَهُ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (Immaculate is He and exalted above [having] any partners they ascribe [to Him]!), is a word of glorification (تنزيه). It is from the speech of Allah and is not part of what the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was told to say. This is because the state of the polytheists in relation to Him is one of address rather than the third person; had it been from the speech of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), it would have been said: "above what you ascribe" (عما تشركون) in the second person.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما كانَ النَّاسُ إِلَّا أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَاخْتَلَفُوا — Mankind were but a single community; then they differed (10:19). It has already been mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) of His saying, the Exalted: كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ — Mankind were but a single community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed. And none differed in it except those who had been given it, after the manifest proofs had come to them, out of envy among themselves (2:213), that the verse reveals two types of difference (الاختلاف) among people.

The first: Difference in terms of livelihood, which relates to legal claims. By it, people are divided into claimant and defendant, oppressor and oppressed, transgressor and transgressed against, the one taking his right and the one whose right is lost. This is what Allah, the Glorified, removed by establishing the religion (الدين), sending the prophets, and revealing the Book with them to judge between people in that which they differed, to teach them the knowledge of religion, and to confront them with warning and glad tidings.

The second: Difference in the religion itself and the true knowledge (المعارف) of the principles and branches contained in the Divine Book. The Quran has explicitly stated in various places that this type of difference originates with the scholars of the Book out of "envy among themselves" (بغياً بينهم); it is not something necessitated by human nature like the first type. By this, the path is divided into the paths of guidance (الهداية) and misguidance (الضلال). Thus, Allah guided those who believed to the truth concerning that about which they differed. He, the Glorified, mentioned in several places of His speech, after mentioning this type of difference, that were it not for a decree from Allah that had preceded, He would have judged between them in that which they differed, but He delays them until a term. He, the Exalted, said: وَما تَفَرَّقُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ — They did not divide except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves; and were it not for a prior word from your Lord [deferring them] until a specified time, it would have been decided between them (42:14), among other verses.

The context of the preceding verse—namely His saying, the Exalted: وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ ما لا يَضُرُّهُمْ وَلا يَنْفَعُهُمْ (They worship besides Allah that which neither harms them nor benefits them)—does not suit either of the two mentioned differences except the second one, which is the difference in the religion itself. This is because it mentions people taking the path of misguidance by worshipping what neither harms nor benefits them and taking intercessors with Allah. This necessitates that the meaning of people previously being "a single community" is their being upon one religion, which is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد); then they differed and split into two groups: monotheist and polytheist.

So Allah mentioned therein that their difference necessitated that Allah judge between them by manifesting the truth over falsehood, which would involve the destruction of the followers of falsehood and the salvation of the followers of truth. However, the preceding Divine Word prevented the judgment between them. That Word is His saying, the Exalted, when He sent man down to the world: وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتاعٌ إِلى حِينٍ — There will be for you on the earth an abode and a provision for a while (2:36).

The commentators have strange views regarding the verse, including: that "mankind" refers to the Arabs, who were upon one true religion, the religion of Abraham (peace be upon him), until the time of Amr ibn Luhayy, who introduced idolatry among them, so they split into monotheistic hunafā’ and polytheistic idol-worshippers. You are well aware that there is absolutely no evidence for this from the wording.

Another view: that "mankind" refers to all of them, and the meaning of their being "a single community" is their being upon the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — of Islam, even if they were always differing. Thus, the word "were" (كان) is stripped of temporal significance, and the verse recounts what people are upon according to nature—which is monotheism—and what they are upon according to actuality—which is difference. Thus, people, according to their primordial nature, are but a single community of monotheists, but they differed in opposition to their nature.

This is problematic because it contradicts the apparent meaning of the verse and the verse in Surah al-Baqarah, as well as the apparent meaning of other verses such as: وَما تَفَرَّقُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ (They did not divide except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves) (42:14) and His saying: وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ (Those who were given the Book did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves) (3:19). Furthermore, the claim of the existence of permanent difference among people while it does not return to the primordial nature is a contradiction.

Another view: that the meaning is that all people were upon a single creed, which was disbelief (الكفر) and polytheism (الشرك), then they differed, so there was the Muslim and the disbeliever.

This is the most absurd of views regarding the verse. In addition to being a claim without evidence, it is rejected by the apparent meaning of the verses. For their apparent meaning is that the emergence of difference—due to its ending in the envy of people after knowledge had come to them, meaning the emergence of disbelief and polytheism out of envy—was what necessitated the judgment between them and the decree against them by the descent of punishment and destruction. If they were all upon disbelief and polytheism without any prior guidance or faith, what would be the meaning of attributing the necessity [of judgment] to envy arising from knowledge? And what would be the meaning of creating everyone and the existence of a necessity to destroy them all except the frustration of the Divine purpose?

This view is more similar to what the Christians say regarding the issue of atonement: that Allah created man to feed him and settle him in Paradise forever, but he disobeyed Him and thereby frustrated the purpose of creation, so Allah rectified it through the atonement of Christ.

Another view: some say that the "word" in His saying, وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ (were it not for a prior word from your Lord...), is His saying, the Exalted, in this Surah: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ — Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ (10:93).

The problem with this is: if the "precedence" (السبق) meant is precedence in terms of statement, then that verse comes after this verse, as it occurs at the end of the Surah, while the verses are connected and flowing. Furthermore, that verse is specifically about the Children of Israel, and the pronoun in "between them" (بَيْنَهُمْ) refers to them; it is His saying: وَلَقَدْ بَوَّأْنا بَنِي إِسْرالعِيلَ مُبَوَّأَ صِدْقٍ وَرَزَقْناهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ فَمَا اخْتَلَفُوا حَتَّى جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ — Certainly We settled the Children of Israel in a worthy settlement and We provided them with all the good things. They did not differ until knowledge came to them. Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ (10:93).

Moreover, His saying in some verses: وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ (were it not for a prior word from your Lord [deferring them] until a specified time, it would have been decided between them) (42:14) does not harmonize with this meaning of precedence.

If the precedence meant is precedence in terms of decree (القضاء), then one should follow in that the first word Allah spoke regarding the misguidance of people, their polytheism, and their disobedience, which is nothing other than what He said when He first settled man on the earth, which is the verse we previously cited.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَقُولُونَ لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ فَقُلْ إِنَّمَا الْغَيْبُ لِلَّهِ فَانْتَظِرُوا إِنِّي مَعَكُمْ مِنَ الْمُنْتَظِرِينَ — They say, ‘Why has not some sign been sent down to him from his Lord?’ Say, ‘[The knowledge of] the Unseen belongs only to Allah. So wait; I too am waiting along with you’ (10:20). This verse, like the one before it, "They worship besides Allah...", and the one before that, "And when Our signs are recited to them...", enumerates matters from the injustices of the polytheists in their words and deeds, then refutes them with arguments dictated to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) so that he may establish them against them, as passed at the beginning of the verses. Thus, His saying, وَيَقُولُونَ لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ (And they say, ‘Why has not...’), is coordinated with His saying at the beginning of the verses: وَإِذا تُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا (And when Our signs are recited to them).

In it, furthermore, there is a return after return to their denial of the matter of the Quran. For their intention in saying, "Why has not some sign been sent down to him from his Lord?", even if it was a request for another sign besides the Quran, they only said it out of disparagement and belittlement of the matter of the Quran and out of disdain for it, by not counting it as a divine sign. The evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted: فَقُلْ إِنَّمَا الْغَيْبُ لِلَّهِ (Say, ‘[The knowledge of] the Unseen belongs only to Allah’). He did not say "Say" (قل) as He did in other verses; it is as if He is saying: They ask you for another sign, not being content with the Quran nor satisfied with it. So if they are not content with it as a sign, then say: Signs are from the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — which is exclusive to Allah and is not in my hand. So wait; I too am waiting along with you.

This is what is benefited from the verse. It contains an indication that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was waiting for a decisive sign between truth and falsehood other than the Quran, judging between him and his community. The explicit promise of this sign—which he is commanded to wait for here—will come in His saying: وَإِمَّا نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ الَّذِي نَعِدُهُمْ أَوْ نَتَوفَّيَنَّكَ فَإِلَيْنا مَرْجِعُهُمْ — Whether We show you a part of what We promise them, or take you away, to Us is their return (10:46) until the completion of several verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا أَذَقْنَا النَّاسَ رَحْمَةً مِنْ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُمْ إِذا لَهُمْ مَكْرٌ فِي آياتِنا — When We let the people taste mercy after some adversity has visited them, behold, they plot against Our signs (10:21) until the end of the verse. Although the content of the verse is among the general meanings applicable to most people at most times—for an individual human is not free from being touched by ease after adversity; rather, it rarely happens that this does not repeat in his case—the verse, from the perspective of the preceding context, is as if it were aimed as an allusion to the polytheists and their plotting against the signs of Allah. The evidence for this is His saying: قُلِ اللهُ أَسْرَعُ مَكْراً (Say, ‘Allah is swifter at plotting’). The focus was directed toward the plotting of a specific group, who are the addressees of these verses, as they used to plot against the signs of ease and adversity after their appearance. Among their plotting was their plotting against the Quran, which is a divine sign and a mercy Allah let them taste after the adversity of ignorance that clung to them, and the encompassing of hardship of life, humiliation, division, and the distance and hatred of hearts for them. Yet they plot against it; sometimes they say, "Bring a Quran other than this or alter it," and sometimes they say, "Why has not some sign been sent down to him from his Lord?"

So the verse clarifies to them that all of this is a plot they are plotting against the signs of Allah, and it clarifies to them that plotting against the signs of Allah results in nothing but evil without benefiting them in any way. For Allah is swifter at plotting; His plot takes them before their plot takes His signs, for their plotting against the signs of Allah is the very plot of Allah against them.

The meaning of the verse: وَإِذا أَذَقْنَا النَّاسَ (And when We let the people taste)—He expressed the affliction as "tasting" (الإذاقة) to hint at their enjoyment of the mercy and to indicate its smallness, for "tasting" is used for a small amount of nourishment. رَحْمَةً مِنْ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُمْ (mercy after some adversity has visited them)—the expression "mercy" in the place of ease is to indicate that it is from the divine mercy without them deserving it. It was thus obligatory upon them to fulfill its right and to submit to what the sign calls for, which is the monotheism of their Lord and gratitude for His blessing. But they surprise with something else: إِذا لَهُمْ مَكْرٌ فِي آياتِنا (behold, they plot against Our signs), such as interpreting events in a way that nullifies the indication of the signs, like their saying, "Adversity and ease touched our fathers," and making excuses that Allah does not approve of, like their saying, "Why has not some sign been sent down to him?", and their saying, "If we follow the guidance with you, we will be snatched away from our land."

So Allah commanded His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to answer them by saying: قُلِ اللهُ أَسْرَعُ مَكْراً (Say, ‘Allah is swifter at plotting’), then He justified it by saying: إِنَّ رُسُلَنا يَكْتُبُونَ ما تَمْكُرُونَ (Indeed Our messengers write down what you plot). For We have over you witnesses and watchers whom We sent to you, who write down your deeds and preserve them. As soon as you have performed an act, it is preserved against you and its recompense is determined for you before your plot takes effect or fails to take effect, as they have interpreted it.

Here is something: the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: هذا كِتابُنا يَنْطِقُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْحَقِّ إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنْسِخُ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — This is Our record; it speaks against you with the truth. Indeed We used to record what you used to do (45:29), according to the explanation that will come in the commentary of the verse, if Allah wills, is that the meaning of the angels writing the deeds of the servants is their bringing the deeds out from the latency of potentials into the stage of external actuality and the inscribing of the deeds themselves in the record of the cosmos. By this, the reason for the messengers writing their deeds being that He, the Exalted, is swifter at plotting becomes completely clear. For the reality of the meaning according to this is: It is We who bring out your deeds with which you plot from within your essences and place them in the external world; so how can it be hidden from Us that you intend to plot against Us by that? And is plotting anything other than diverting another from what he intends through a ruse and concealment from him? Rather, that which you claim to be a plot against Us is a plot from Us against you, as We make you claim it to be a plot and proceed to plot against Us. This claim and proceeding is a misguidance from you and a leading astray from Us for you as a recompense for what your hands have earned. A similar meaning will come in His saying: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّما بَغْيُكُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ — O mankind! Your rebellion is only against yourselves (10:23).

In the verse, there is a transition (التفات) from the third person to the second person in His saying: إِنَّ رُسُلَنا يَكْتُبُونَ ما تَمْكُرُونَ (Indeed Our messengers write down what you plot), according to the reading of tamkurūn (تمكرون) with the tā’ of address, which is the famous reading. This is among the wondrous transitions occurring in the Quran. Perhaps the point in it is to represent the meaning of His saying, "Say, ‘Allah is swifter at plotting’" before the eye. It is as if He, the Exalted, when He said to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), "Say, ‘Allah is swifter at plotting’," wanted to clarify it to them visually, so He surprised them with His manifestation to them suddenly, spoke to them, and clarified to them the reason for His being swifter at plotting. Then He veiled them from Himself, so they returned to their state of being in the third person, and the discourse returned to its state, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was addressed with the rest of the discourse: هُوَ الَّذِي يُسَيِّرُكُمْ (It is He who carries you...), and this is among the subtle transitions.

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي يُسَيِّرُكُمْ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ حَتَّى إِذا كُنْتُمْ فِي الْفُلْكِ وَجَرَيْنَ بِهِمْ — It is He who carries you across land and sea. When you are in the ships, and they sail with them... (10:22) until the end of the verse. The word al-fulk (الفلك) means ship, and it is used for both singular and plural. What is intended here is the plural, as evidenced by His saying, "and they sail with them" (وَجَرَيْنَ بِهِمْ). The "stormy wind" (الريح العاصف) is the one that blows violently. His saying, "they are besieged" (أُحِيطَ بِهِمْ), is a metaphor for being on the brink of destruction; its estimation is that calamity or waves have surrounded them. The indication in His saying, "from this" (مِنْ هذِهِ), is to the hardship. The meaning of the verse is clear.

In it, there is a wondrous transition from address to the third person in His saying: وَجَرَيْنَ بِهِمْ بِرِيحٍ طَيِّبةٍ (and they sail with them with a fair breeze) until His saying, "without any right" (بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِ). Perhaps the point in it is returning them to the third person and directing the address to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and describing the most wondrous part of this story described to him so that he may hear it and wonder at it. In it, there is also a turning away from the matter of addressing them because they do not understand the speech.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا أَنْجاهُمْ إِذا هُمْ يَبْغُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِ — But when He delivers them, behold, they commit outrages on the earth unjustly (10:23). The root of al-baghy (البغي) is seeking, and it is frequently used in the context of injustice (الظلم) because it is the seeking of another's right by transgressing against him. It is then restricted by "without right" (بغير الحق); had it meant pure injustice, the restriction would be redundant.

The sentence is part of the completion of the previous verse. The whole—namely His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي يُسَيِّرُكُمْ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ (It is He who carries you across land and sea) until His saying, "without any right"—is in the position of a witness and an example in relation to the generality of His saying before it: وَإِذا أَذَقْنَا النَّاسَ رَحْمَةً مِنْ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُمْ (When We let the people taste mercy after some adversity has visited them) until the end of the verse, or specifically for His saying: قُلِ اللهُ أَسْرَعُ مَكْراً (Say, ‘Allah is swifter at plotting’). In any case, His saying: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّما بَغْيُكُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ (O mankind! Your rebellion is only against yourselves) is that upon which the completion of the purpose of the discourse in the previous verse depends, even if it is not from the speech of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). So understand that.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّما بَغْيُكُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ مَتاعَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا ثُمَّ إِلَيْنا مَرْجِعُكُمْ — O mankind! Your rebellion is only against yourselves. [It is] the enjoyment of the life of this world; then to Us will be your return (10:23) until the end of the verse. In the discourse, there is a transition from the third person to address. Thus, His saying, "O mankind!", etc., is an address from Him, the Exalted, to the people without an intermediary; it is not from the speech of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) among what Allah commanded him to address the people with.

The evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ إِلَيْنا مَرْجِعُكُمْ (then to Us will be your return) until the end of the verse, for it is not suitable to be from the address of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

The point in this transition is similar to the point we previously mentioned in His saying, the Exalted, at the beginning of the discourse: إِنَّ رُسُلَنا يَكْتُبُونَ ما تَمْكُرُونَ (Indeed Our messengers write down what you plot). It is as if He, the Glorified, surprises them by observing them while the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) addresses them, and they think that their Lord is absent from them, heedless of their intentions and purposes in their deeds. So He oversees them and represents by that His being with them in all their states and His encompassing them. He says to them: I am closer to you and to your deeds than you are. What you perform of deeds by which you intend to rebel against Us and plot against Us only exists by Our decree and proceeds by Our hands; so how can you rebel by them against Us? Rather, it is a rebellion from you against yourselves, for it distances you from Us and its sins are written in the records of your deeds. So your rebellion is against yourselves, and it is the enjoyment of the life of this world (متاع الحياة الدنيا) which you enjoy for a few days; then to Us is your return, and We will inform you and clarify to you there the realities of your deeds.

His saying, مَتاعَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا (the enjoyment of the life of this world), is in the accusative case (النصب) in the reading of Hafs from Asim, and the estimation is: you enjoy the enjoyment of the life of this world. It is in the nominative case (الرفع) in the reading of others, and it is the predicate of a deleted subject, the estimation being: it—meaning your rebellion and your deed—is the enjoyment of the life of this world.

In both readings, His saying, "the enjoyment of the life of this world" until the end of the verse, is a detailing of the brevity of His saying: "Your rebellion is only against yourselves." Thus, His saying, "enjoyment," etc., stands in the position of justification (التعليل) for their rebellion being against themselves, of the type of justifying the brief with the detailed and clarifying it by it.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما مَثَلُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا كَماءٍ أَنْزَلْناهُ مِنَ السَّماءِ فَاخْتَلَطَ بِهِ نَباتُ الْأَرْضِ — The parable of the life of this world is that of water that We send down from the sky: with it the flora of the earth... (10:24) until the end of the verse. When He, the Glorified, mentioned in the previous verse the enjoyment of the life of this world, He struck for it this parable in which He describes the reality of its matter so that those who take heed may consider. It is of the nature of a representational metaphor (استعارة تمثيلية) and is not the comparison of a single thing to another single thing, even if His saying, "is that of water that We send down," initially gives that impression. Its likes are common in the parables of the Quran. Al-zukhurf (الزخرف) is ornament and splendor. His saying, لَمْ تَغْنَ (as if it had not flourished), is from ghaniya (غني) in a place when one resides in it and prolongs the stay. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ يَدْعُوا إِلى دارِ السَّلامِ وَيَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ — Allah invites to the Abode of Peace, and He guides whomever He wishes to a straight path (10:25). Al-du‘ā’ (الدعاء) and al-da‘wah (الدعوة) — the act of calling or inviting — is the turning of the attention of the one called toward what he is called to and the drawing of his focus. It is more general than "calling out" (النداء), for calling out is specific to the domain of wording and sound, while invitation can be through wording, gesture, and other means. Calling out is only done loudly, while invitation is not restricted by that.

Invitation from Allah, the Glorified, is ontological (تكويني), which is the bringing into existence of what He intends for a thing as if He were calling it to what He intends. He, the Exalted, said: يَوْمَ يَدْعُوكُمْ فَتَسْتَجِيبُونَ بِحَمْدِهِ — The day He calls you, and you respond with His praise (17:52), meaning He calls you to the afterlife and you respond by accepting it. It is also legislative (تشريعي), which is the charging of people with what He intends of religion through the tongue of His signs. Invitation from the servant to his Lord is the turning of His mercy and care toward himself by placing himself in the position of servitude (العبودية) and being owned (المملوكية). Therefore, worship (العبادة) is in reality an invitation (دعاء), because the servant therein places himself in the position of the owned and of connection to his Master through following and humility, so that He may turn toward him with His mastership and lordship, and that is the invitation.

To this, His saying, the Exalted, points: وَقالَ رَبُّكُمُ ادْعُونِي أَستَجِبْ لَكُمْ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِي سَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ داخِرِينَ — Your Lord has said, ‘Call Me, and I will hear you!’ Indeed those who are too proud to worship Me will enter hell, abased (40:60), where He first expressed it as "calling" (الدعاء) then substituted it secondly with "worship" (العبادة).

The matter became confused for the author of al-Manār, who said in his commentary: "The saying of some commentators and others that among the meanings of al-du‘ā’ is worship is not correct in its absolute sense regarding the legislative, obligatory worship. For fasting is not called du‘ā’ linguistically or legislatively. Rather, du‘ā’ is the marrow of primordial worship and the greatest of the pillars of the obligatory part of it, as mentioned in the hadith. So every legislative du‘ā’ is worship, but not every legislative worship is du‘ā’." End quote. The source of his error was his claim that the meaning of al-du‘ā’ is calling out for a request and his heedlessness of the analysis of its meaning mentioned above.

The origin of the meaning of al-salām (السلام), according to what al-Rāghib mentioned in al-Mufradāt, is being free from outward and inward defects. To this, its meaning returns in all its derivatives. Al-salām and al-salāmah are one, like al-radā‘ and al-radā‘ah. It is apparent that al-salām and "security" (الأمن) are close in meaning; the only difference is that al-salām is security taken in itself, while security is al-salām in relation to what one is safe from. It is said: he is in salām, and he is in amn from such and such.

Al-Salām is among His names, the Exalted, because His transcendent essence is the very good in which there is no evil. Paradise is called the Abode of Peace (دار السلام) because there is no evil in it nor harm for its inhabitant. It is said: it was only named the Abode of Peace because it is the abode of Allah, who is al-Salām. The result is one in reality, because He, the Exalted, was only named Salām due to His freedom from every evil and ill. In the context of the verse, there is that which suggests that the descriptive meaning of salām is intended in the discourse.

Allah, the Glorified, has left al-salām absolute and did not restrict it by anything, nor has anything appeared in His speech that restricts it to certain aspects. Thus, it is the Abode of Peace in an absolute sense, and it is nothing other than Paradise. For what exists with us in the world of peace is only relative rather than absolute; there is nothing except that it is crowded and prevented from some of what it loves and desires, and there is no state except that it has concomitants of opposites and rivals.

So if you take the meaning of salām as absolute and not relative, you obtain what Paradise possesses of description, and it is revealed that its being described with this attribute is similar to its being described in His saying: لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ فِيها — They will have therein whatever they wish (50:35). For the peace (سلامة) of man from everything he dislikes and does not love necessitates his authority over everything he wishes and loves.

In the restriction of the Abode of Peace by its being "with their Lord," there is an indication of the proximity of presence and their lack of heedlessness of Him, the Glorified, there at all. The discussion on the meaning of guidance (الهداية) and the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — has already passed in various places of the previous discussions, such as the commentary of Surah al-Hamd and others.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: قالَ الَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ لِقاءَنَا ائْتِ بِقُرْآنٍ غَيْرِ هذا (those who do not expect to encounter Us say, ‘Bring a Quran other than this...’): He said: "For Quraysh said: 'O Messenger of Allah, bring us a Quran other than this, for this is something you learned from the Jews and the Christians.' Allah said: 'Say to them: Had Allah wished, I would not have recited it to you, nor would He have made it known to you. I have certainly lived out forty years among you before revelation was sent to me, and I did not speak a word of it until it was revealed to me.'"

I say: In the correspondence of its content to the verse, there is some obscurity, in addition to what it contains of them addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with the messengership.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyāshī, narrated from Mansūr ibn Hāzim from Abu ‘Abdillāh (peace be upon him), he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) did not cease saying: إني أخاف إن عصيت ربي عذاب يوم عظيم — 'Indeed I fear, if I should disobey my Lord, the punishment of a tremendous day' — until Surah al-Fath was revealed; then he did not return to that speech."

I say: The narration is not without some issues.

In al-Durr al-Manthūr: al-Bayhaqī recorded in al-Dalā’il from ‘Urwah, who said: "‘Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl fled on the day of the Conquest [of Mecca] and took to the sea. A wind caught him, so he called out to al-Lāt and al-‘Uzzā. The people of the ship said: 'No one here is permitted to call upon anything except Allah alone, with sincerity.' ‘Ikrimah said: 'By Allah, if He is alone in the sea, He is alone on the land.' So he embraced Islam."

I say: The narration is reported through many different paths.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyāshī, narrated from Mansūr ibn Yūnus from Abu ‘Abdillāh (peace be upon him): "ثلاث يرجعن على صاحبهن: النكث والبغي والمكر — 'Three things return upon their doer: breaking an oath (النكث), rebellion (البغي), and plotting (المكر).' Allah said: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّما بَغْيُكُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ (O mankind! Your rebellion is only against yourselves)."

I say: It is narrated from Anas from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who said: "ثلاث هن رواجع على أهلها: النكث والمكر والبغي — 'Three things are returners upon their people: breaking an oath, plotting, and rebellion.'" Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّما بَغْيُكُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ (O mankind! Your rebellion is only against yourselves), وَلا يَحِيقُ الْمَكْرُ السَّيِّئُ إِلَّا بِأَهْلِهِ (but the evil plot only enmeshes its own authors), and فَمَنْ نَكَثَ فَإِنَّما يَنْكُثُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ (so whoever breaks his oath, breaks it only to his own detriment). It was cited in al-Durr al-Manthūr.

In al-Durr al-Manthūr: Abu Nu‘aym recorded in al-Hilyah from Abu Ja‘far Muhammad ibn ‘Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "ما من عبادة أفضل من أن تسأل، وما يدفع القضاء إلا الدعاء، وإن أسرع الخير ثوابا البر، وأسرع الشر عقوبة البغي، وكفى بالمرء عيبا أن يبصر من الناس ما يعمى عليه من نفسه، وأن يأمر الناس بما لا يستطيع التحول عنه، وأن يؤذي جليسه بما لا يعنيه — 'There is no worship better than that you ask [of Allah], and nothing repels the decree except invitation (الدعاء). Indeed, the swiftest good in reward is righteousness (البر), and the swiftest evil in punishment is rebellion (البغي). It is enough of a defect for a man that he sees in people what he is blind to in himself, that he commands people with what he cannot turn himself toward, and that he harms his companion with what does not concern him.'"

In it, Ibn Mardawayh recorded from Ibn ‘Abbās, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: لو بغى جبل على جبل لدك الباغي منهما — 'If a mountain were to rebel against a mountain, the rebellious one of them would be crushed.'"

In the Tafsir of al-Burhān, from Ibn Bābawayh with his chain from al-‘Alā’ ibn ‘Abd al-Karīm, who said: "I heard Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) saying regarding the word of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَاللهُ يَدْعُوا إِلى دارِ السَّلامِ (Allah invites to the Abode of Peace): He said: 'إن السلام هو الله عز وجل وداره التي خلقها لأوليائه الجنة — "Indeed, al-Salām is Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and His abode which He created for His friends is Paradise."'"

In it, from Ibn Shahrāshūb from ‘Ali ibn ‘Abdillāh ibn ‘Abbās from his father and Zayd ibn ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him): regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ يَدْعُوا إِلى دارِ السَّلامِ (Allah invites to the Abode of Peace): "Meaning by it Paradise; وَيَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ (and He guides whomever He wishes to a straight path): meaning the mastership (ولاية) of ‘Ali ibn Abi Tālib."

I say: If the narration is suspended (موقوفة), then it is of the nature of "application" (الجري) or from the inner meaning (الباطن) of the Quran. There are other narrations with its meaning.

Ayahs

۞ لِّلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ وَزِيَادَةٌ ۖ وَلَا يَرْهَقُ وُجُوهَهُمْ قَتَرٌ وَلَا ذِلَّةٌ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٦)
Those who are virtuous shall receive the best reward and an enhancement. Neither dust nor abasement shall overcast their faces. They shall be the inhabitants of paradise, and they shall remain in it [forever]. (26)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَسَبُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ جَزَآءُ سَيِّئَةٍۭ بِمِثْلِهَا وَتَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ ۖ مَّا لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ عَاصِمٍ ۖ كَأَنَّمَآ أُغْشِيَتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ قِطَعًا مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ مُظْلِمًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٧)
For those who have committed misdeeds, the requital of a misdeed shall be its like, and they shall be overcast by abasement. They shall have no one to protect [them] from Allah. [They will be] as if their faces were covered with dark patches of the night. They shall be the inmates of the Fire, and they shall remain in it [forever]. (27)
وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ مَكَانَكُمْ أَنتُمْ وَشُرَكَآؤُكُمْ ۚ فَزَيَّلْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ وَقَالَ شُرَكَآؤُهُم مَّا كُنتُمْ إِيَّانَا تَعْبُدُونَ (٢٨)
On the day when We gather them all together, We shall say to those who ascribe partners [to Allah], ‘Stay where you are—you and your partners!’ Then We shall set them apart from one another, and their partners will say, ‘It was not us that you worshipped. (28)
فَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًۢا بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمْ إِن كُنَّا عَنْ عِبَادَتِكُمْ لَغَـٰفِلِينَ (٢٩)
Allah suffices as a witness between you and us. We were indeed unaware of your worship.’ (29)
هُنَالِكَ تَبْلُوا۟ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّآ أَسْلَفَتْ ۚ وَرُدُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَوْلَىٰهُمُ ٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٣٠)
There every soul will examine what it has sent in advance, and they will be returned to Allah, their real master, and what they used to fabricate will forsake them. (30)

(Commentary)

This is a new discourse returning to the mention of the recompense for deeds and the return of all to Allah, the Truth (الحق). An allusion to this has already preceded, and within it is the establishment of the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His creation, sovereignty, and governance of the universe.

His saying, the Exalted: لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا الْحُسْنَى وَزِيَادَةٌ وَلَا يَرْهَقُ وُجُوهَهُمْ قَتَرٌ وَلَا ذِلَّةٌ “For those who do good is the best reward, and an extra. Neither dust nor abasement shall overcast their faces.” (10:26). Al-Husna (الحسنى) is the feminine form of ahsan (أحسن), and what is meant is the best reward (المثوبة الحسنى). What is meant by "extra" (الزيادة) is that which exceeds what is deserved, based on the fact that Allah, out of His grace (فضل), has made for every deed a certain measure of recompense and reward, then made it a right for the worker, as in His saying: لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ “they shall have their reward near their Lord” (3:199). Then He multiplied it and made the multiplied portion also a right for the worker, as in His saying: مَنْ جَاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثَالِهَا “Whoever brings a good deed shall receive ten times its like” (6:160). At that point, the import of His saying, “For those who do good is the best reward,” is their entitlement to the best recompense and reward, and the "extra" would be the increase over the amount deserved—whether the single like or the ten likes—similar to the meaning conveyed by His saying: فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ وَيَزِيدُهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ “As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, He will pay them their rewards in full, and enhance them out of His grace.” (4:173).

If what is meant by al-Husna in His saying, “For those who do good is the best reward,” were the "best end" (العاقبة الحسنى), and since there is nothing intellectually conceivable above the "best," then the meaning of His saying, “and an extra,” would be an increase over what a human can conceive of divine grace (الفضل الإلهي). This is indicated by His saying: فَلَا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ مَا أُخْفِيَ لَهُمْ مِنْ قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ “No one knows what has been kept restorative for them” (32:17), and what is in His saying: لَهُمْ مَا يَشَاءُونَ فِيهَا وَلَدَيْنَا مَزِيدٌ “There they will have whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more.” (40:35). For it is known that every good thing a human desires is surpassed by the "more" (المزيد) which is beyond what he can perceive. So understand that.

The term al-rahaq (الرهق) refers to overtaking or covering. It is said: "Debt overtook (rahaqahu) him," meaning it caught up to him and covered him. Al-qatar (القتر) is black smoke or black dust. Describing them with His saying, “Neither dust nor abasement shall overcast their faces,” is a parallel to the description of the people of the Fire in the following verse regarding the blackness of their faces with qatar—which is a physical blackness—and abasement (الذلة)—which is a spiritual blackness.

The meaning is: For those who did good in the world is the best reward and an extra from the grace of Allah—or the best end and an extra that does not cross their minds—and no blackness from dust or abasement shall cover their faces; “those are the inhabitants of paradise; they shall remain in it [forever].”

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ كَسَبُوا السَّيِّئَاتِ جَزَاءُ سَيِّئَةٍ بِمِثْلِهَا وَتَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ “As for those who have committed vices, the requital of a vice shall be its like, and they shall be overcast by abasement.” (10:27) to the end of the verse. The phrase “the requital of a vice shall be its like” (جَزَاءُ سَيِّئَةٍ بِمِثْلِهَا) is a subject (مبتدأ) for an omitted predicate (خبر), the estimation being: "For them is the requital of a vice with its like of punishment." This entire sentence is the predicate for the subject which is His saying, “As for those who have committed vices.” The meaning is that those who earned vices are not recompensed except with the like of the evil punishments they committed; thus, the requital for an evil act is an evil punishment.

His saying: مَا لَهُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ مِنْ عَاصِمٍ “They shall have no one to protect them from Allah” means they have no protector to shield them from Allah, i.e., from His punishment. In this is a negation of their partners (شركائهم) whom they imagine to be intercessors (شفعاء), in a manner that negates every preventing protector, whether it be a partner-intercessor, a powerful opposing preventer, or any protector other than them.

His saying: كَأَنَّمَا أُغْشِيَتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ قِطَعًا مِنَ اللَّيْلِ مُظْلِمًا “as if their faces were covered by layers of the dark night” (10:27). Qita‘an (قطعاً) is the plural of qit‘ah (قطعة) — a piece or segment, and muthliman (مظلماً) — dark is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for "the night." The meaning is as if the dark night were divided into segments and those segments were cast over their faces, making them completely black. The immediate sense is that the face of each of the polytheists is covered by one of those segments, not as some have interpreted it to mean that the faces were covered by those segments one after another, resulting in layers of darkness one above the other; for there is nothing in the speech to indicate that.

His saying: أُولَئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ “They shall be the inmates of the Fire, and they shall remain in it [forever]” indicates the permanence of their stay in the Fire, as the terms "inmates" (أصحاب) and "immortality" (الخلود) denote this, just as its counterpart regarding the inhabitants of Paradise indicates the same for them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا مَكانَكُمْ أَنْتُمْ وَشُرَكاؤُكُمْ “On the day We shall muster them all together, then We shall say to the polytheists, ‘Stay in your place, you and your partners!’” (10:28) to the end of the verse. What is meant is the gathering (حشر) of all those previously mentioned—the believers, the polytheists, and their partners. For He, the Exalted, mentions the polytheists and their partners in this verse and the one following it, then refers to everyone by His saying in the subsequent verse: هُنالِكَ تَبْلُوا كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما أَسْلَفَتْ “There every soul will examine what it has sent in advance.”

His saying: ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا مَكانَكُمْ أَنْتُمْ وَشُرَكاؤُكُمْ “then We shall say to the polytheists, ‘Stay in your place, you and your partners!’” means: "Remain in your place, you, and let your partners remain in their place." Following this address, "We shall set them apart from one another" (فَزَيَّلْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ), severing the bond that used to tie them to their partners—which was the bond of illusion (الوهم) and assumption (الحسبان) through which they connected to their partners. Thus, they were cut off from their partners and their partners from them, and it became clear that their worship (عبادتهم) did not actually fall upon them nor relate to them, because they only worshipped "partners," and those were not truly partners.

The evidence for what we have mentioned is His saying, the Exalted, immediately after: وَقالَ شُرَكاؤُهُمْ ما كُنْتُمْ إِيَّانا تَعْبُدُونَ “and their partners will say, ‘It was not us that you used to worship.’” (10:28). The speech is to be taken according to its literal sense as a serious and truthful negation of their worship of them. They are not lying in this speech, as evidenced by their reliance on the testimony of Allah, the Glorified. Nor do they mean "we did not call you to worship us," for the wording does not suit this meaning. Nor is their intention to taunt them by saying "you were actually worshipping your own desires and the satans who misled you," for that does not suit their claim of being unaware (الغفلة). Likewise, it is not suited by His saying afterward: هُنالِكَ تَبْلُوا كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما أَسْلَفَتْ “There every soul will examine what it has sent in advance,” according to the meaning that will follow. Rather, their intention is to negate the worship in reality by negating the reality of the partnership (الشركة), and citing as evidence for that the testimony of Allah and His knowledge of their unawareness of their worship.

Worship (العبادة) — the act of ultimate submission and devotion — which is a connection involving being owned (المملوكية) and humility (التذلل) from the worshipper toward the worshipped, only becomes worship if it connects and links to the worshipped—so that the meaning of the preposition "for" (اللام) is fulfilled in our saying: "worship is for him." This cannot occur except with the awareness (شعور) of the worshipped and his knowledge of it. If knowledge is not realized there, then worship is not realized in reality; it is merely the form of worship (صورة عبادة).

It has thus become clear that the meaning of His saying, “then We shall say to the polytheists, ‘Stay in your place, you and your partners!’ then We shall set them apart from one another,” is His manifestation and disclosure, the Exalted, on that day of the reality of the matter which illusions had covered and desires had veiled in the world. That reality is that the true mastership (المولوية) and the ownership of the realm of governance (مالكية زمان التدبير) belong to Allah, the Glorified, and nothing else possesses any mastership or lordship (ربوبية) such that seeking refuge in it would be valid or worship of it would be true.

When Allah removes the veil from the face of this reality on that day, it will become clear to the polytheists that their partners were not truly partners nor were they worshipped by them in reality—due to their unawareness of their worship—and that they were only performing for them a form of worship which illusion and desire depicted as being worship, when it was not.

To this also points the saying of the Exalted: وَإِذَا رَأَى الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا شُرَكاءَهُمْ قالُوا رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ شُرَكاؤُنَا الَّذِينَ كُنَّا نَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِكَ فَأَلْقَوْا إِلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلَ إِنَّكُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ “When the polytheists see their partners, they will say, ‘Our Lord! These are our partners whom we used to invoke besides You.’ But they will retort at them, ‘You are indeed liars!’” (16:86).

It has also become clear from this that His saying, “and their partners will say, ‘It was not us that you used to worship,’” is a statement from their partners to them in earnest and truth. This reveals the error of the view of some who say: "The meaning is: 'You did not worship us by our command or our call,' not that you did not worship us at all, because that would be a lie, and lying is not permitted to occur in the Hereafter because they are compelled therein to abandon what is foul."

For the negation of the essence of worship, according to the meaning you have come to know, is the absolute truth. Affirming the worship, while not a lie, is not free from a degree of metaphor (مجاز) when considering the reality of the matter. Furthermore, what he mentioned—that the meaning is the negation of worship by their command and call—is a meaning for which there is no evidence from the wording.

Moreover, lying only does not occur in the Hereafter if it is an act and an acquisition (كسبا); as for it being a result of worldly dispositions (الملكات), there is no barrier to its possibility. In fact, it does occur, as He, the Exalted, narrates in His saying: ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُنْ فِتْنَتُهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا وَاللَّهِ رَبِّنا ما كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ انْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَذَبُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ “Then their only excuse will be to say, ‘By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists.’ See how they lie against themselves” (6:23-24), and other verses.

Similarly, the view of some who say: "The meaning is: 'You did not exclusively worship us; rather, you were worshipping your desires, your lusts, and your satans who misled you.'" For the fact that worshipping desires and Satan is true of their action—from the perspective that it is a following of desire and Satan—does not negate the fact that it is also truly a worship of idols. Allah affirms all three perspectives in His speech; He, the Exalted, says: وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ ما لا يَضُرُّهُمْ وَلا يَنْفَعُهُمْ “They worship besides Allah that which neither harms them nor benefits them” (10:18); and He says: أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلَهَهُ هَواهُ “Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god?” (45:23); and He says: أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ “that you should not worship Satan? He is indeed your manifest enemy.” (36:60).

It is well known that the partners are arguing to negate their being worshipped by them, not to prove that desire and Satan were worshipped along with the partners; for this would not benefit them in the argument at all, and it would render their affirmation of unawareness for themselves futile in their saying: إِنْ كُنَّا عَنْ عِبادَتِكُمْ لَغافِلِينَ “we were indeed unaware of your worship.” (10:29). This is because desires also were not conscious of their worship, just as the idols, being dead bodies, were not.

Perhaps the proponent of this view relied on the restriction (الحصر) understood from His saying: ما كُنْتُمْ إِيَّانا تَعْبُدُونَ “It was not us that you used to worship,” by placing the object before its verb. Its literal sense is a "reversal restriction" (قصر قلب) whose import is the negation of being worshipped from themselves and its affirmation for others, not a negation of the essence of worship; for they affirm it in their saying: “of your worship” (عَنْ عِبادَتِكُمْ), as the addition of the verbal noun (المصدر) to its object implies its existence.

However, the truth is that these partners only said to them, “It was not us that you used to worship,” in response to what the polytheists said, as narrated by Allah: رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ شُرَكاؤُنَا الَّذِينَ كُنَّا نَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِكَ “Our Lord! These are our partners whom we used to invoke besides You.” (16:86). Thus, they negated their worship of Allah, the Glorified, and affirmed it for the partners. The partners had no interest except to negate the worship of the polytheists from themselves. As for whom it was affirmed for, they had no purpose related to that; rather, their concern was to disassociate themselves from the claim of partnership. They argued for this by affirming unawareness (الغفلة) of that for themselves; for if they had been conscious of their worship and had accepted them as worshippers, it would have necessitated that they—the partners—were claiming partnership.

His saying, the Exalted: فَكَفى بِاللَّهِ شَهِيداً بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمْ “Allah suffices as a witness between us and you” (10:29) to the end of the verse. Its meaning has become clear through the preceding explanation. The fa (ف) in His saying, “So Allah suffices” (فَكَفى بِاللَّهِ), provides the justification (التعليل), like our saying: "Worship Allah, for He is your Lord," and this is common in speech.

His saying, the Exalted: هُنالِكَ تَبْلُوا كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما أَسْلَفَتْ “There every soul will examine what it has sent in advance” (10:30) to the end of the verse. Al-bala’ (البلاء) is testing or examination (الاختبار). The indication in His saying, “There” (هُنالِكَ), is to the station (الموقف) He mentioned by His saying: “then We shall say to the polytheists, ‘Stay in your place, you and your partners!’ then We shall set them apart from one another.”

That station is a station where every soul will test and examine what it has sent in advance and performed of deeds. The reality of its deeds will be disclosed to it, and it will witness them with its own eyes—not merely through memory or description. By witnessing the truth (الحق) of everything directly, it will be revealed that the True Master (المولى الحق) is Allah, the Glorified. All illusions will fall and crumble, and all the claims that man fabricated through his illusions and desires against the Truth will vanish.

These fabrications and claims only arose from the connections we establish in this world between causes and effects, and the independence and mastership we grant to causes. Yet there is no god but Allah, and no true master but He, the Glorified. When the reality of the matter is unveiled, the cloud of illusion is dispersed, and the veil of claims is torn, it will appear that there is no true master but He, the Glorified. All the deities that were only established by human fabrication will be nullified, and all deeds will fall and be rendered void, except those through which Allah, the Glorified, was worshipped with true worship.

Thus, the three segments of the verse—namely His saying, “every soul will examine...”, His saying, “they shall be returned to Allah...”, and His saying, “and that which they used to fabricate will forsake them”—each helps the others in conveying the reality of its meaning. The sum total of their import is the manifestation of the reality of Divine Guardianship (الولاية الإلهية) on that day as a direct witnessing, and that nothing besides Him, the Exalted, possesses anything but pure poverty and being owned. At that point, every false claim will be nullified and the edifice of illusions will be demolished.

As indicated by His saying: هُنالِكَ الْوَلايَةُ لِلَّهِ الْحَقِّ “There, guardianship belongs only to Allah, the Real.” (18:44); and His saying: يَوْمَ هُمْ بارِزُونَ لا يَخْفى عَلَى اللَّهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ “The day when they will emerge [from their graves], nothing about them will be hidden from Allah. ‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’” (40:16); and His saying: وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ “and on that day all command will belong to Allah.” (82:19), among others.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Amali al-Mufid, with its chain to Abu Ishaq al-Hamdani from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): In what he wrote to Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr when he appointed him over Egypt—and commanded him to read it to the people, and in what he wrote: Allah the Exalted said: لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا الْحُسْنَى وَزِيَادَةٌ — and al-Husna is Paradise, and the "extra" is the world.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "As for al-Husna, it is Paradise; and as for the 'extra', it is the world—what Allah gave them in the world, Allah will not hold them to account for in the Hereafter, and Allah will gather for them the reward of the world and the Hereafter." (The tradition).

I say: The two narrations are looking at the first meaning we presented in the preceding commentary. Al-Tabarsi narrated something with the meaning of the second in al-Majma‘ from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him).

In Tafsir al-Burhan, it is narrated in Nahj al-Bayan from Ali ibn Ibrahim that he said: "The 'extra' is the gift of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Daraqutni and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Suhayb regarding the verse: He said: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: "The 'extra' is looking at the face of Allah."

I say: This meaning is narrated through several chains from the sources of the Sunnis from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny). The clarification of its meaning has already preceded in the commentary on the saying of the Exalted: رَبِّ أَرِنِي أَنْظُرْ إِلَيْكَ “My Lord, show [Yourself] to me, that I may look at You!” (7:143) in the eighth volume of this book.

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abu Basir from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): Regarding His saying: كَأَنَّما أُغْشِيَتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ قِطَعاً مِنَ اللَّيْلِ مُظْلِماً — he said: "Do you not see the house when it is night—it is more intensely black than the outside—likewise their faces increase in blackness."

I say: Al-Ayyashi narrated it from Abu Basir from him (peace be upon him). It is as if he (peace be upon him) intends to interpret the "segments of the night" (القطع من الليل) mentioned in the verse.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu al-Shaykh extracted from al-Suddi: Regarding His saying: وَرُدُّوا إِلَى اللَّهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِّ — he said: "It was abrogated by His saying: مَوْلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَأَنَّ الْكافِرِينَ لا مَوْلى لَهُمْ ('[That is] because Allah is the protector of the faithful, and because the faithless have no protector')."

I say: This is among the most absurd of statements. Rather, the two verses look at two different aspects of meaning, which are the apparent (الظاهر) and the hidden (الباطن).

Ayahs

قُلْ مَن يَرْزُقُكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ أَمَّن يَمْلِكُ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ وَمَن يُخْرِجُ ٱلْحَىَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَيِّتِ وَيُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَيِّتَ مِنَ ٱلْحَىِّ وَمَن يُدَبِّرُ ٱلْأَمْرَ ۚ فَسَيَقُولُونَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ فَقُلْ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (٣١)
Say, ‘Who provides for you out of the sky and the earth? Who controls [your] hearing and sight, and who brings forth the living from the dead and brings forth the dead from the living, and who directs the command?’ They will say, ‘Allah.’ Say, ‘Will you not then be wary [of Him]?’ (31)
فَذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۖ فَمَاذَا بَعْدَ ٱلْحَقِّ إِلَّا ٱلضَّلَـٰلُ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُصْرَفُونَ (٣٢)
That, then, is Allah, your true Lord. So what is there after the truth except error? Then where are you being led away? (32)
كَذَٰلِكَ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ فَسَقُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٣٣)
Thus the word of your Lord became due against those who transgress, that they shall not have faith. (33)
قُلْ هَلْ مِن شُرَكَآئِكُم مَّن يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ ۚ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُؤْفَكُونَ (٣٤)
Say, ‘Is there anyone among your partners who originates the creation and then brings it back?’ Say, ‘Allah originates the creation, then He will bring it back.’ Then where do you stray? (34)
قُلْ هَلْ مِن شُرَكَآئِكُم مَّن يَهْدِىٓ إِلَى ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يَهْدِى لِلْحَقِّ ۗ أَفَمَن يَهْدِىٓ إِلَى ٱلْحَقِّ أَحَقُّ أَن يُتَّبَعَ أَمَّن لَّا يَهِدِّىٓ إِلَّآ أَن يُهْدَىٰ ۖ فَمَا لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ (٣٥)
Say, ‘Is there anyone among your partners who may guide to the truth?’ Say, ‘Allah guides to the truth. Is He who guides to the truth worthier to be followed, or he who is not guided unless shown the way? What is the matter with you? How do you judge?’ (35)
وَمَا يَتَّبِعُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ إِلَّا ظَنًّا ۚ إِنَّ ٱلظَّنَّ لَا يُغْنِى مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ شَيْـًٔا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا يَفْعَلُونَ (٣٦)
Most of them just follow conjecture; indeed conjecture is no substitute for the truth. Indeed Allah knows best what they do. (36)

(Commentary)

These are radiant proofs for His monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — in Lordship (الربوبية), which He commands His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to establish against the polytheists (المشركين). These are three arguments arranged according to their subtlety and robustness. The first argument proceeds from the path considered by the pagans and idol-worshippers; for they only worship the lords of the idols through their idols because of their governance (تدبير) of the universe. Thus, they worship each of them for the specific affair assigned to it and the governance (التدبير) attributed to it, so that it may be pleased with its worshipper and bestow its blessings upon him, or to grant him security so it does not send its wrath and punishment upon him. This is similar to how coastal dwellers used to worship the lord of the sea, and the people of the mountains, the people of the land, the people of sciences and crafts, and the people of wars and raids, and others—each worshipping the one whose governance (تدبير) suited the affair that concerned them, so that their lord might be pleased with them and bless them through his pleasure, or withhold his anger from them.

The gist of the argument is that the governance (تدبير) of the human world and all other existing things is carried out by Allah, the Glorified, and none other, as they themselves admit. Therefore, it is mandatory that they single Him out in Lordship (الربوبية) and worship none but Him.

The second argument is what the general body of believers considers. This is because they do not pay much heed to the trappings of this world (النشأة) and its material pleasures; rather, their primary concern is the eternal life of the Hereafter, whose felicity or wretchedness is determined by the divine recompense (الجزاء) for their deeds. Thus, when the rational evidence (البينة العقلية) is established for the Return (الإعادة) being like the Beginning (البدء), it becomes mandatory that none be worshipped except Allah, the Glorified, and that no lords be taken besides Him out of desire for His reward or fear of His punishment.

The third argument is the one toward which the hearts of the elite (الخاصة) among the believers yearn. It is that what must be followed according to reason (العقل) is the Truth (الحق). Since the Truth, the Glorified, is the One who guides to the Truth, unlike the lords they invoke besides Allah, then He must be the one followed rather than the lords they invoke. A clarification of these three arguments will follow in the commentary on the verses, through which they will become even more manifest, if Allah wills.

Were it not for the consideration of this subtle point, it would have been apparent to mention the second argument first, then the third, then the first; or to mention the second and then combine the first and third to be mentioned after it.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ مَنْ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَمَّنْ يَمْلِكُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصارَ — Say, ‘Who provides for you from the sky and the earth? Who controls [your] hearing and sight?’ (10:31) until the end of the verse. Provision (الرزق) is the continuous bestowal. Allah’s providing for the human world from the sky is the descent of rain, snow, and the like; and from the earth, it is by its bringing forth vegetation and its nurturing of animals, and from both of these, man receives provision. By the blessing of these divine favors, the human species survives. The meaning of "controlling hearing and sight" is His being the Disposer (المتصرف) of the human senses through which various types of enjoyment from the different provisions are organized for man—provisions which Allah has permitted him to enjoy. For man identifies and distinguishes what he desires from what he does not desire by employing hearing, sight, touch, taste, and smell; thus, he moves toward what he desires and stops or flees from what he dislikes through them.

Thus, the senses are that through which the benefit of divine provision is completed. Hearing and sight were specifically mentioned among them because their effects in vital activities are more apparent than others. Allah, the Glorified, is the One who owns them and disposes of them by giving, withholding, increasing, and decreasing.

And His saying: وَمَنْ يُخْرِجُ الْحَيَّ مِنَ الْمَيِّتِ وَيُخْرِجُ الْمَيِّتَ مِنَ الْحَيِ — ‘and who brings forth the living from the dead, and brings forth the dead from the living?’ (10:31). Life (الحياة), according to the initial view regarding man, is the principle (المبدأ) through which knowledge (العلم) and power (القدرة) manifest in a thing, such that its actions proceed from knowledge and power as long as life remains; and when it ceases, that proceeding likewise ceases.

Then, it was discovered through scientific inquiry (النظر العلمي) that this is not restricted to the categories of animals, as the initial view suggested. For the criterion (الملاك) that necessitated the animal being possessor of life—which is its being possessor of a soul (نفس) from which various actions proceed, not in a single natural pattern, such as its movement toward different directions with different motions and its stillness without movement—is present in plants.

Likewise, current research following modern methods confirms this; for the germs of life present in the animal, to which its vital functions trace back, have their counterparts in the plant. Thus, it possesses life just like the animal. Scientific inquiry, in any case, leads to the universality of life for all species of animals and plants.

Furthermore, life (الحياة)—which is the opposite of death (الموت), the latter being the cessation of the principle of vital functions—returns, according to analysis, to a thing being such that its desired effects can follow from it, just as death is its not being so. Thus, the life of the earth is its being productive and green, and its death is the opposite. The life of an action is its being such that it leads to the purpose for which it was performed, and its death is the opposite. The life of a word is its being such that it produces a desired effect in the listener, and its death is the opposite. The life of a human is his proceeding according to what the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — guides to, such as his being possessor of a sound intellect (عقل سليم) and a pure soul (نفس زاكية). For this reason, the Noble Quran considered religion (الدين) to be life for man, because it views the true religion, which is Islam, as the divine primordial nature (الفطرة).

Once this is clear, it becomes evident that the meaning of bringing forth the living from the dead and the dead from the living varies according to the intended meaning of life and death. According to the first two views, it is the emergence of the animal, or the animal and plant, into being from something else, such as sperm, the egg, and the seed. For just as this life does not endure for the living thing eternally in its survival, it also does not trace back at its beginning to an infinite life, nor is there a way to prove such a thing. It also refers to the emergence of non-living parts from the animal, or the animal and plant, by separation.

According to the last view—meaning the view of generalizing life to everything from which its desired effects follow—it is to bring forth useful matters from useless matters in that domain through being and generation, such as the creation of the living human, the living animal, and the living plant from the dead earth, and vice versa; and like the emergence of the rational, righteous human from the human who has no intellect or righteousness, and vice versa; and the emergence of the believer (المؤمن) from the disbeliever (الكافر) and the disbeliever from the believer.

The apparent meaning of the noble verse, considering its context and the station of address therein, is that the intended meaning of bringing forth the living from the dead and vice versa is this latter meaning. This is because the verse establishes the argument against the polytheists from the path they used to take in arguing for the adoption of various deities. Their argument was that the witnessed world is a collection of different and scattered existents, celestial and terrestrial; and the terrestrial include humans, animals, plants, sea, land, and many things beyond that. Each of these is under the governance (تدبير) of a governor (مدبر) who is an intercessor (شفيع) with Allah, whom we worship by worshipping his idol so that he may bring us closer to Allah in station. In short, the tracing back of various governances to different governors necessitates the existence of many lords besides Allah.

The verse refutes their argument by explaining that the various governances trace back to Him, the Exalted, and that this indicates that Allah, the Glorified, is the Lord (رب) of everything alone. It addresses them saying: You admit that the governance (التدبير) specific to you, such as your provision, and that which is general to you and others, traces back to Allah, the Glorified. Thus, He is the Governor (المدبر) of your affair and the affair of others; therefore, He is the Lord (الرب), and there is no lord besides Him.

It began the enumeration with what is specific to man, namely His saying: قُلْ مَنْ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ — Say, ‘Who provides for you from the sky and the earth?’ and concluded with what is general to him and others, namely His saying: وَمَنْ يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ — ‘and who directs the command?’ The apparent context suggests that the intended meaning of His saying: أَمَّنْ يَمْلِكُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصارَ وَمَنْ يُخْرِجُ الْحَيَّ مِنَ الْمَيِّتِ — ‘Who controls [your] hearing and sight? And who brings forth the living from the dead’ is the governance specific to man. Thus, the meaning would be the ownership of the hearing and sight belonging to human individuals, and likewise the bringing forth of the living from the human from a dead state and vice versa. It has already been clarified that the life specific to man is his being possessor of the blessing of intellect and religion.

So, the meaning of bringing forth the living from the dead and vice versa—and Allah knows best—is bringing forth the human who is alive with human felicity (السعادة) from the dead human who has no felicity, and vice versa.

Thus, Allah, the Glorified, instructs His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in the argument for His monotheism in Lordship, commanding him by His saying: قُلْ — Say that he should say to them in the context of interrogation: مَنْ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ — ‘Who provides for you from the sky and the earth?’ through rain, vegetation, and creation; أَمَّنْ يَمْلِكُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصارَ — ‘Who controls [your] hearing and sight?’ among you, so that the benefit of your provision is completed through them, as you receive provision by identifying through them the good things of provision; were it not for them, you would not have succeeded in that and would have perished to the last of you; وَمَنْ يُخْرِجُ الْحَيَّ مِنَ الْمَيِّتِ — ‘and who brings forth the living from the dead,’ meaning every useful matter in its domain from another; وَيُخْرِجُ الْمَيِّتَ مِنَ الْحَيِ — ‘and brings forth the dead from the living?’ so the felicitous human is born from the wretched, and the wretched from the felicitous; وَمَنْ يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ — ‘and who directs the command?’ in all of creation.

فَسَيَقُولُونَ اللهُ — They will say, ‘Allah.’ (10:31) in admission that He is the One to whom all these governances in man and others trace back, because the pagans believe that. Then the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was commanded to first rebuke them for abandoning godwariness (تقوى الله) — the state of being conscious of Allah's presence and avoiding His displeasure — by worshipping others despite the clarity of the proof, and then to conclude for them from the proof the necessity of His monotheism, so He said: فَقُلْ أَفَلا تَتَّقُونَ — Say, ‘Will you not then be godwary?’ Then He said: فَذلِكُمُ اللهُ رَبُّكُمُ — That, then, is Allah, your true Lord.

His saying, the Exalted: فَذلِكُمُ اللهُ رَبُّكُمُ الْحَقُّ فَما ذا بَعْدَ الْحَقِّ إِلَّا الضَّلالُ فَأَنَّى تُصْرَفُونَ — That, then, is Allah, your true Lord. So what is there after the truth except error? Then where are you being seconded? (10:32). The first sentence is the result of the preceding argument. The Lord has been described as "the True" (الحق) to serve as a clarification of the content of the argument and as a preparation and preamble for His saying after it: فَما ذا بَعْدَ الْحَقِّ إِلَّا الضَّلالُ — So what is there after the truth except error?

His saying: فَما ذا بَعْدَ الْحَقِّ إِلَّا الضَّلالُ — So what is there after the truth except error? takes the necessary implication of the previous argument to conclude that they are astray in worshipping idols. For if His Lordship, the Exalted, is true, then guidance (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — lies in following and worshipping Him; for guidance is with the truth and nothing else, so nothing remains with other than it, which is falsehood (الباطل), except error (الضلال).

The implied meaning of the speech is: "What is there after the Truth with which there is guidance, except Falsehood with which there is error?" Thus, something was omitted from each of the two sides and the remainder was established in its place for the sake of conciseness (إيجاز). It was said: "What is there after the Truth except error?" For this reason, some have said that there is ihtibāk (احتباك) in the verse—which is one of the rhetorical beauties (المحسنات البديعية)—where there are two opposites and something is omitted from each of them that the other indicates. For the implied meaning of the speech is: "What is there after the Truth except Falsehood? And what is there after Guidance except Error?" Thus, "Falsehood" was omitted from the first and "Guidance" from the second, and there remained His saying: "What is there after the Truth except error?" The correct view is what we presented first.

Then He completed the verse with His saying: فَأَنَّى تُصْرَفُونَ — Then where are you being seconded? meaning: until when will you be turned away from the Truth with which there is guidance, toward the error which is with falsehood?

His saying, the Exalted: كَذلِكُ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ فَسَقُوا أَنَّهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ — Thus has the word of your Lord become due against those who are transgressors: that they shall not believe. (10:33). The apparent context is that the "word" (الكلمة) which Allah, the Glorified, spoke against the transgressors (الفاسقين) is that they shall not believe. That is, He, the Glorified, decreed a final decree (قضاء حتما) against them, which is that the transgressors—while they remain in their transgression—shall not believe and the divine guidance to faith shall not reach them. He, the Exalted, has said: وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ — and Allah does not guide the transgressing lot. (5:108).

Accordingly, the indication in His saying: كَذلِكَ — Thus is to what was obtained from the previous verse: that the polytheists were turned away from the Truth and transgressed from it, so they fell into error, since there is nothing after the Truth except error.

So the meaning of His saying: كَذلِكَ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ — Thus has the word of your Lord become due etc., is that the divine word and the final decree which was decreed regarding the transgressors—which is that they shall not believe—has thus become due and established in external reality and has taken its instance (مصداق), which is that they departed from the Truth and fell into error. That is, We did not decree the lack of guidance for the transgressors and their lack of faith out of injustice or haphazardly; rather, We decreed that because they turned away from the Truth and transgressed, so they fell into error, and there is no middle ground between them. Understand this.
