In the verse, there is an indication that necessary matters and the clear judgments and laws that run in the witnessed system—such as our saying: "There is no middle ground between truth and falsehood, nor between guidance and error"—have a type of dependence on the divine decree (القضاء الإلهي), and are not established in His kingdom, the Exalted, of their own accord.

Some commentators have mentioned that the intended meaning of "the word" in the verse is the word of punishment, and His saying: أَنَّهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ — that they shall not believe is in the position of causal explanation (التعليل) by assuming the particle lām. The implied meaning would be: "Just as this proof is established against them, the word of your Lord has become due against those who transgressed, which is the threat of punishment against them; and the punishment has only become due against them because they do not believe."

This is not free from weakness, for the point of resemblance is not apparent nor agreed upon in both. The proof is established against them by its very essence, whereas the punishment—its establishment is not like that, but rather for another reason, which is that they do not believe.

The argument—as you heard in the preceding commentary—is a simple argument whose truth the pagans admit, yet they turned it away from its proper face and established it upon what they claim of the lordship of their lords and their worthiness of worship besides Allah, when they said: "The governance of every affair among the general affairs of the world belongs to one of these lords, so he is the lord of that affair; and we only worship their idols and statues to please them thereby, so they may intercede for us with Allah by virtue of their proximity to Him."

So the verse took their admission that these governances belong to Allah, the Glorified—and how could they not belong to Him when He is the Creator of all and their Sustainer?—so to Him, the Glorified, alone belongs the reality of Lordship, and He is the One worthy of worship, none other.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هَلْ مِنْ شُرَكائِكُمْ مَنْ يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ — Say, ‘Is there anyone among your partners who originates the creation and then brings it back?’ (10:34) until the end of the verse. This is an instruction for argumentation from the perspective of the Beginning (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد). For the One who originates everything and then brings it back is worthy of man worshipping Him out of wariness of the day of meeting Him, so that he may be safe from His painful punishment and attain His great reward on the Day of Return.

Since the polytheists—who are the ones addressed by the argument—did not believe in the Return, Allah, the Exalted, commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to undertake the answer to his own question himself, saying: قُلِ اللهُ يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ فَأَنَّى تُؤْفَكُونَ — Say, ‘Allah originates the creation and then He brings it back.’ Then where are you being seconded? and until when will you be turned away from the Truth?

The verse's reliance on the issue of Origination and the Return in its argument is not a reliance on a premise that is neither clear nor explained. For He has argued for it in His speech, the Exalted, through various ways, such as arguing from the necessity of a purpose (الغاية) in His act, and from the necessity of recompense for deeds in justice, and other ways. He, the Glorified, has negated doubt regarding the Resurrection (البعث) and the Day of Judgment in about ten places in His speech.

The argument—as previously alluded to—is the argument of the general body of believers who worship Him, the Exalted, out of fear of punishment or desire for the reward prepared for them on the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هَلْ مِنْ شُرَكائِكُمْ مَنْ يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ قُلِ اللهُ يَهْدِي لِلْحَقِ — Say, ‘Is there anyone among your partners who guides to the truth?’ Say, ‘Allah guides to the truth.’ (10:35) until the end of the verse. "Guiding to the truth" (يهدي للحق) and "guiding toward the truth" (إلى الحق) carry the same meaning; for guidance (الهداية) is made transitive by both particles. Its transitiveness with the lām has occurred in many places in His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying: أَوَلَمْ يَهْدِ لَهُمْ — Does it not guide them... (32:26), and His saying: يَهْدِي لِلَّتِي هِيَ أَقْوَمُ — guides to that which is most upright (17:9), among others. Thus, what some have mentioned regarding the lām in His saying: يَهْدِي لِلْحَقِ being for causal explanation is of no consequence.

He, the Glorified, instructed His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in this argument, which is the third of the arguments. It is a rational argument (حجة عقلية) upon which the elite among the believers rely. Its clarification is that it is embedded in the human primordial nature (الفطرة), and by it his intellect judges, that it is mandatory for man to follow the Truth. So much so that if he deviates in any of his actions from the Truth and follows something else due to error, doubt, or passion, he only followed it because he thought it was the Truth and the matter was confused for him. Therefore, he excuses himself with what he thinks is the Truth. Thus, the Truth is mandatory to follow absolutely and without any restriction or condition.

The one who guides to the Truth must be followed because of the Truth he possesses. It is mandatory to prefer him over one who does not guide to it or guides to something else, because following the one who guides to the Truth is following the Truth itself, the following of which is a necessary obligation.

The argument relied on this necessary premise, so it opened the speech by asking them about their partners: is there among them anyone who guides to the Truth? It is clear that the polytheists have no affirmative answer to that; for their partners—whether they be inanimate objects without life like idols and statues, or whether they be among the living like angels, the lords of species, the jinn, and the tyrants (الطواغيت) like Pharaoh, Nimrod, and others—do not possess for themselves any harm or benefit, nor death, nor life, nor resurrection.

Since they have no affirmative answer to that, they do not answer. Therefore, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was commanded to take their place in answering, so he answers regarding that—meaning the guidance to the Truth—by affirming it for Allah, the Glorified. So it was said: قُلِ اللهُ يَهْدِي لِلْحَقِ — Say, ‘Allah guides to the truth.’ For Allah, the Glorified, is the One who guides everything to its ontological goals (مقاصده التكوينية) and the matters it needs for its survival, as in His saying: رَبُّنَا الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى — ‘Our Lord is He who gave everything its creation and then guided it.’ (20:50) and His saying: الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى — who created and proportioned, who determined and guided (87:2-3). He is the One who guides man to the felicity of life and calls him to Paradise and forgiveness by His permission, by sending messengers, revealing books, and legislating laws, and commanding them to spread the true religious call among the people.

It has already been mentioned in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ فَلا تَكُنْ مِنَ الْمُمْتَرِينَ — The truth is from your Lord, so do not be among the doubters. (3:60) that the Truth in belief, speech, and action only becomes true by its correspondence to the established system in the universe, which is His act. Thus, the Truth in reality only becomes true by His will and volition.

Once it is established that none of their partners guides to the Truth, and that Allah, the Glorified, guides to the Truth, He asked them by His saying: أَفَمَنْ يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ أَحَقُّ أَنْ يُتَّبَعَ أَمَّنْ لا يَهِدِّي إِلَّا أَنْ يُهْدى — ‘Is He who guides to the truth worthier to be followed, or he who guides not unless he [himself] is guided?’ (10:35) that they should judge in the preference between following Him, the Exalted, and following their partners—while He, the Exalted, guides to the Truth and they do not guide, nor are they guided except by another. It is well known that the preference belongs to the one who guides over the one who does not guide—meaning His being followed, the Exalted, over their being followed. Yet the polytheists judge the opposite. Therefore, He blamed and rebuked them by His saying: فَما لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ — What is the matter with you? How do you judge?

The expression of preference in His saying: أَحَقُّ أَنْ يُتَّبَعَ — worthier to be followed using the superlative form (أفعل التفضيل), which indicates absolute preference rather than exclusivity and restriction—even though following Him, the Exalted, is the Truth and nothing else, and following them has no share of the Truth—is only in consideration of the station of preference and to make it easier for them to accept the statement without provoking their partisanship or agitating their ignorance.

Allah has shown exquisite precision in His saying: أَفَمَنْ يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ أَحَقُّ أَنْ يُتَّبَعَ أَمَّنْ لا يَهِدِّي إِلَّا أَنْ يُهْدى. The common recitation is لا يَهِدِّي with a kasrah on the hā’ and a shaddah on the dāl, and its origin is yahtadī (يهتدي). The apparent meaning of His saying: لا يَهِدِّي إِلَّا أَنْ يُهْدى —while the objects of the verb have been omitted—is that he is only guided by another, not by himself.

In the speech, His saying: يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِ — guides to the truth has been contrasted with His saying: أَمَّنْ لا يَهِدِّي — or he who guides not. While "guiding to the truth" is contrasted with "not guiding to the truth," and "not being guided to the truth" is contrasted with "being guided to the truth," the implication of this contrast is the concomitance between being guided by another and not guiding to the truth. Likewise, the concomitance between guiding to the truth and being guided by one's own essence. Thus, the one who guides to the truth must be guided by himself, not by the guidance of another; and the one who is guided by another never guides to the truth.

This is what the verse indicates according to its apparent meaning, in which there is no doubt. It is the fairest witness that the speech therein is based on reality (الحقيقة), rather than the metaphorical usages based on laxity that we, the people of common usage (أهل العرف), base ourselves upon and circulate among us—where we attribute "guiding to the truth" to anyone who speaks a word of truth and calls to it, even if he does not believe in it, or believes but does not act upon it, or acts but has not realized its meaning; and whether he was guided to it by himself or another guided him to it.

Rather, guiding to the truth—meaning the act of bringing one to the explicit truth and the core of reality—belongs only to Allah, the Glorified, or to one who is guided by himself, meaning Allah, the Glorified, guided him without any intermediary intervening between Him and him; so he was guided by Allah and guided others by the command of Allah, the Glorified. A portion of the discussion on this meaning has already preceded under His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذِ ابْتَلى إِبْراهِيمَ رَبُّهُ بِكَلِماتٍ فَأَتَمَّهُنَّ — And when his Lord tested Abraham with certain words, and he fulfilled them... (2:124).

From what we have presented regarding the meaning of the verse, several matters have become clear:

First: That the intended meaning of "guidance to the truth" is that which is in the sense of bringing one to the goal (الإيصال إلى المطلوب), rather than that which is in the sense of showing the path (إراءة الطريق) that leads to the truth. For it is necessary that the description of the path of truth can come from anyone, whether he was guided to the truth by himself or by another, or was not guided at all.

Second: That the intended meaning of His saying: أَمَّنْ لا يَهِدِّي إِلَّا أَنْ يُهْدى is the one who is not guided by himself. This is more general than being among those who are guided by another or being among those who are not guided at all—neither by themselves nor by another—such as idols and statues which are inanimate objects that do not accept guidance from another. This is because His saying: إِلَّا أَنْ يُهْدى is an exception from His saying: أَمَّنْ لا يَهِدِّي, which is more general than not being guided at all or being guided by another. What is taken in His saying: أَنْ يُهْدى is a verb preceded by the infinitive-forming an (أن المصدرية), which is interpreted as a verbal noun (المصدر). A verbal sentence interpreted as a verbal noun in this way does not indicate actualization (التحقق), unlike a verbal noun added to its object. Thus, there is a difference between His saying: أَنْ تَصُومُوا خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ — That you fast is better for you (2:184), which does not indicate occurrence, and something like His saying: إِنْ كُنَّا عَنْ عِبادَتِكُمْ لَغافِلِينَ — we were indeed unaware of your worship (10:29), which indicates occurrence. It is said: "Your striking Zayd is amazing" when you have struck him, and "That you strike Zayd is amazing" when you have intended to strike him.

So His saying: أَمَّنْ لا يَهِدِّي إِلَّا أَنْ يُهْدى means: the one whose guidance is not from himself unless guidance comes to him from the direction of another. It is well known that it only comes to him from another if it is in his nature to accept that. But if he does not accept it, then only the description remains for him that he is not guided. Understand this.

The commentators have strange opinions regarding the meaning of this exception:

Among them: That it is a "voided exception" (استثناء مفرغ) from the most general of states, because those from whom guidance was negated among those taken as partners to Allah include the Messiah Jesus son of Mary, Ezra, and the angels (عليه السلام). These used to guide to the truth by the guidance of Allah and His revelation, as He, the Exalted, said regarding the prophets in their Surah: وَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا — We made them imams, guiding by Our command (21:73).

The critique of this: Its gist is that the meaning is "he does not guide unless Allah guides him, so he guides others after being guided by the guidance of Allah, the Exalted." This has corrupted the meaning of the verse from its foundation; for the one who is not guided to the truth by himself is not able to guide to the truth, for he only touches the truth from behind a veil, so how can he bring others to it?

Furthermore, what he mentioned does not apply to the idols which are the subject of the argument in the verse, for they do not accept guidance at all. He mentioned the Messiah and Ezra, who are among those sanctified by the Christians and Jews, but the direction of the speech in the verse is not toward them, even if the verse includes them and others according to the generality of the criterion.

Among them: That the exception is "disjunctive" (منقطع) and the intended meaning of "the one who does not guide" is the idols which do not accept guidance at all, and nothing else. The meaning would be: "Or the one who is not guided at all like the idols, unless Allah guides him, then he would be guided then."

The critique of this: It does not suffice to justify the contrast between His saying: مَنْ يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِ and His saying: أَمَّنْ لا يَهِدِّي. For "guiding to the truth" and "being guided to it" are not contrasted unless the meaning is interpreted as something like our saying: "Is He who guides to the truth worthier to be followed, or he who is not guided at all unless Allah guides him, so he is guided and then guides others?" The same objection as the previous one applies: there would be no reason then to restrict it to the likes of idols among those who are not guided at all so that the exception becomes disjunctive; rather, it would generalize to what is not guided at all—neither by itself nor by another—and what is not guided by itself but is guided by another, like the angels for example. And what applied to the previous view applies to this one.

Among them: That the intended meaning of "the one who does not guide" is the idols which do not accept guidance, and إِلَّا (unless) is in the sense of hattā (until). The meaning would be: "he is not guided and does not accept guidance until he is guided."

The critique of this: The alternative then returns to something like our saying: "Is He who guides to the truth worthier to be followed, or he who is not guided at all until he is guided to the truth?" The exception becomes redundant and no purpose in the speech relates to it. Additionally, the usage of illā in the sense of hattā is not established, and even if it were, it is rare in speech and the most eloquent of speech is not interpreted based on the likes of it.

Among them: That the intended meaning of "the one who does not guide unless he is guided" is the angels and the jinn among those worshipped besides Allah, and they accept guidance from Allah even if they are not guided from themselves. Or the intended meaning is the misguiding leaders who call to disbelief; for even if they are not guided, they accept guidance, and if they were guided to the truth, they would have been guided to it.

The critique of this: The verses occur in the context of the argument against the idol-worshippers. The claim that the intended meaning of "the one who does not guide unless he is guided" is the angels and the jinn or the misguiding leaders removes it from the suitability of applying to the case at hand.

Third: That guiding to the truth in the sense of bringing one to it (الإيصال إليه) is only the affair of one who is guided by himself—meaning there is no intermediary between him and Allah, the Glorified, in the matter of guidance, either from the beginning of his affair or by a special grace from Allah, the Glorified, such as the prophets and the successors (الأوصياء) among the Imams. As for guidance in the sense of showing the path and describing the way, it is not restricted to Him, the Exalted, nor to the Imams among the prophets and successors, as Allah, the Exalted, narrates from the believer of the family of Pharaoh when he says: وَقالَ الَّذِي آمَنَ يا قَوْمِ اتَّبِعُونِ أَهْدِكُمْ سَبِيلَ الرَّشادِ — The one who had believed said, ‘O my people! Follow me, I will guide you to the way of rectitude.’ (40:38) and He said: إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَإِمَّا كَفُوراً — Indeed We have guided him to the way, be he grateful or ungrateful. (76:3).

As for His saying, the Exalted, addressing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while he is an Imam: إِنَّكَ لا تَهْدِي مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ — You cannot guide whomever you wish, but Allah guides whomever He wishes (28:56) and other verses, they are aimed at explaining the original (الأصالة) and the derivative (التبع), as in the verses of taking souls (التوفي), knowledge of the Unseen (علم الغيب), and the like, which were aimed at explaining that Allah, the Glorified, is the One who possesses them by essence and reality, and others possess them by Allah’s granting possession in a derivative or accidental manner, and they become a cause for them by Allah’s permission. He, the Exalted, said: وَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا — We made them imams, guiding by Our command (21:73). In the narrations (الأحاديث), there is an indication of that, and that guiding to the truth is the affair of the Prophet and his Household (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Some discussion on guidance has already preceded.

His saying at the end of the verse: فَما لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ — What is the matter with you? How do you judge? is an interrogation for wonder (التعجيب), expressing astonishment at their judgment to follow their partners despite the explicit judgment of reason that it is impermissible to follow one who is not guided and does not guide to the truth.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما يَتَّبِعُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ إِلَّا ظَنًّا إِنَّ الظَّنَّ لا يُغْنِي مِنَ الْحَقِّ شَيْئاً — Most of them just follow conjecture; indeed conjecture is no substitute for the truth. (10:36). "Sufficed" (أغنى) is made transitive by both min and ‘an. It has appeared in the divine speech in both ways; it was made transitive by min as in the verse, and by ‘an as in His saying: ما أَغْنى عَنِّي مالِيَهْ — My wealth did not avail me. (69:29).

The following of conjecture was attributed to "most of them" because the minority of them—who are the leaders of misguidance—are certain of the truth, yet they did not prefer falsehood over it and call to it except out of rebelliousness (بغياً), as He, the Exalted, said: وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ — and none differed about it except those who had been given it, after the manifest proofs had come to them, out of envy among themselves. (2:213). As for the majority, they only followed their fathers in imitation (تقليداً) of them due to their good opinion of them.

His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ عَلِيمٌ بِما يَفْعَلُونَ — Indeed Allah knows best what they do. is a causal explanation for His saying: وَما يَتَّبِعُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ إِلَّا ظَنًّا. The meaning is that Allah is knowledgeable of the deeds they perform; He knows that they are a following of conjecture.

Ayahs

وَمَا كَانَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانُ أَن يُفْتَرَىٰ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِن تَصْدِيقَ ٱلَّذِى بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَتَفْصِيلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٣٧)
This Quran could not have been fabricated by anyone besides Allah; rather, it is a confirmation of what was [revealed] before it, and an elaboration of the Book, there is no doubt in it, from the Lord of all the worlds. (37)
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ ۖ قُلْ فَأْتُوا۟ بِسُورَةٍ مِّثْلِهِۦ وَٱدْعُوا۟ مَنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٣٨)
Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Then bring a surah like it, and invoke whomever you can, besides Allah, should you be truthful.’ (38)
بَلْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِمَا لَمْ يُحِيطُوا۟ بِعِلْمِهِۦ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِهِمْ تَأْوِيلُهُۥ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ كَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٣٩)
Indeed, they deny something whose knowledge they do not comprehend, and whose explanation has not yet come to them. Those who were before them denied likewise. So observe how was the fate of the wrongdoers! (39)
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يُؤْمِنُ بِهِۦ وَمِنْهُم مَّن لَّا يُؤْمِنُ بِهِۦ ۚ وَرَبُّكَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٤٠)
Some of them believe in it, and some of them do not believe in it, and your Lord best knows the agents of corruption. (40)
وَإِن كَذَّبُوكَ فَقُل لِّى عَمَلِى وَلَكُمْ عَمَلُكُمْ ۖ أَنتُم بَرِيٓـُٔونَ مِمَّآ أَعْمَلُ وَأَنَا۠ بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٤١)
If they deny you, say, ‘My deeds belong to me and your deeds belong to you: you are absolved of what I do and I am absolved of what you do.’ (41)
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَسْتَمِعُونَ إِلَيْكَ ۚ أَفَأَنتَ تُسْمِعُ ٱلصُّمَّ وَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (٤٢)
There are some of them who prick up their ears at you. But can you make the deaf hear even if they do not exercise their reason? (42)
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَنظُرُ إِلَيْكَ ۚ أَفَأَنتَ تَهْدِى ٱلْعُمْىَ وَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يُبْصِرُونَ (٤٣)
There are some of them who observe you. But can you guide the blind even if they do not perceive? (43)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَظْلِمُ ٱلنَّاسَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلنَّاسَ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (٤٤)
Indeed Allah does not wrong people in the least; rather, it is people who wrong themselves. (44)
وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ كَأَن لَّمْ يَلْبَثُوٓا۟ إِلَّا سَاعَةً مِّنَ ٱلنَّهَارِ يَتَعَارَفُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ قَدْ خَسِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِلِقَآءِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُهْتَدِينَ (٤٥)
On the day He will gather them [it will be] as if they had not remained [in the world] except for an hour of the day getting acquainted with one another. They are certainly losers who deny the encounter with Allah, and they are not guided. (45)

(Commentary)

This is a return to the subject of the Quran: that it is a Book revealed from Allah, in which there is no doubt, and a formal instruction (تلقين) on the argument regarding this. These verses are connected to what preceded them in His saying: قُلْ هَلْ مِنْ شُرَكائِكُمْ مَنْ يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ قُلِ اللهُ يَهْدِي لِلْحَقِ "Say, ‘Is there anyone among your partners who guides to the truth?’ Say, ‘Allah guides to the truth.’" (10:34). It has already been mentioned that part of His guidance toward the truth is His guiding people to His religion, which He approves of, through the path of revelation (الوحي) to His prophets and the Books He revealed to them—such as the Books of Nuh, Ibrahim, Musa, ‘Isa, and Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم. These verses mention this and establish the proof that the Quran is among those [Books] that guide to the truth. For this reason, they are alluded to alongside it where it is said: وَلكِنْ تَصْدِيقَ الَّذِي بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَتَفْصِيلَ الْكِتابِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ "but it is a confirmation of what was [revealed] before it, and an elaboration of the Book, there is no doubt in it, from the Lord of all the worlds." (10:37).

At the end of these verses, there is a return to the mention of the Gathering (الحشر) — the resurrection and assembly of all beings for judgment — which is one of the primary objectives of the Surah, as previously noted.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما كانَ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ أَنْ يُفْتَرى مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ "This Quran could not have been fabricated by anyone besides Allah" (10:37) to the end of the verse. It has been previously noted that negating an attribute or a meaning by negating "being" (الكون) implies the negation of the very capacity (الشأن) and potential (الاستعداد) for it. This is more emphatic than merely negating the act itself. There is a difference between saying, "Zayd was not one to stand" (ما كان زيد ليقوم), and saying, "Zayd did not stand" (لم يقم) or "Zayd has not stood" (ما قام زيد). The first indicates that standing was not within Zayd’s capacity, nor was he prepared for it, while the second merely negates the act of standing from him. There are many instances of this in the Quran, such as His saying: فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ "But they would not believe what they had denied before." (10:74), and His saying: ما كُنْتَ تَدْرِي مَا الْكِتابُ وَلَا الْإِيمانُ "You did not know what the Book is, nor what is faith" (42:52), and His saying: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ "Allah would not wrong them" (29:40).

Thus, His saying: وَما كانَ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ أَنْ يُفْتَرى مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ is a negation of the very possibility of fabrication (الافتراء) regarding the Quran, as has been said, and it is more emphatic than negating its actual occurrence. The meaning is: It is not within the nature of this Quran, nor within its capacity, to be a fabrication from other than Allah that someone invents against Allah, the Glorified.

And His saying: وَلكِنْ تَصْدِيقَ الَّذِي بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ "but it is a confirmation of what was [revealed] before it" (10:37); that is, a confirmation of what is present and revealed of the Book, namely the Torah (التوراة) and the Gospel (الإنجيل), just as He narrated from the Messiah عليه السلام his saying: يا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ إِنِّي رَسُولُ اللهِ إِلَيْكُمْ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيَّ مِنَ التَّوْراةِ "O Children of Israel! Indeed I am the apostle of Allah to you, confirming what is before me of the Torah" (61:6). He described them as being "before it" (بين يديه) despite their temporal precedence because there were other Books besides these two, such as the Book of Nuh and the Book of Ibrahim عليه السلام. When the precedence of all of them is considered, those closest to it in time—the Torah and the Gospel—are described as being "before it."

It has been suggested that "what is before it" refers to future events such as the Resurrection (البعث), the Gathering (النشور), the Reckoning (الحساب), and the Recompense (الجزاء), but this is of no consequence.

And His saying: وَتَفْصِيلَ الْكِتابِ "and an elaboration of the Book" (10:37) is conjoined to "confirmation" (تصديق). What is meant by "the Book" (الكتاب), as indicated by the context, is the genus of the Heavenly Book revealed from Allah, the Glorified, to His prophets. Elaboration (التفصيل) — the act of distinguishing and explaining the constituent parts of a whole — is the creation of a distinction between its parts that were integrated into one another or folded within each other, through clarification and explanation.

In this, there is an indication that the Divine Religion (الدين الإلهي) revealed to His prophets عليه السلام is one; there is no difference within it except in terms of brevity (الإجمال) and elaboration (التفصيل). The Quran elaborates what others expressed briefly, as the Exalted said: إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِنْدَ اللهِ الْإِسْلامُ "Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam" (3:19).

Furthermore, the Noble Quran elaborates what was brief in the previous Heavenly Books and is a guardian (مهيمن) over them all, as the Exalted said: وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَمُهَيْمِناً عَلَيْهِ "We have sent down to you the Book with the truth, confirming what was before it of the Book and as a guardian over it" (5:48). And His saying: لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ "there is no doubt in it, from the Lord of all the worlds" (10:37); meaning, there is no doubt that it is from the Lord of all the worlds. The second clause serves as a justification (تعليل) for the first.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Then bring a surah like it’" (10:38) to the end of the verse. The particle "or" (أَمْ) is disjunctive (منقطعة), meaning: "Nay, they say he fabricated it." The pronoun refers to the Quran. Describing a single Surah (سورة) as being "like the Quran" is evidence that the term "Quran" applies to both a large portion and a small portion of it.

The meaning is: Say to those who say he fabricated it: "If you are truthful in your claim, then bring a single Surah like this 'fabricated' Quran, and call upon whomever you can besides Allah for aid and support." For if it were a fabricated speech, it would be human speech, and it would be possible to produce its like. In this, there is an explicit challenge (تحد) with a single Surah from the Surahs of the Quran, whether long or short.

From this, several points become clear. First: the challenge is not restricted to a specific Surah, for they did not accuse only a part of the Quran of being a fabrication to the exclusion of others, but rather the whole of it. He charges them to bring a Surah like that which they claim he fabricated—and they claimed this regarding the entire Quran, not just a part.

One should not heed the opinion of those who say that the indefiniteness (التنكير) in "a Surah" (بِسُورَةٍ) is for magnification (التعظيم) or categorization (التنويع), meaning a Surah among those that mention the stories of the prophets and reports of the warnings of this world and the Hereafter—arguing that fabrication is only suspected in informative reports (الإخبار) rather than performative speech (الإنشاء). Nor should one heed those who say it refers to a long Surah like this one—Surah Yunus—in its inclusion of the foundations of religion, the promise, and the threat.

This is because the Quran, in all its verses, is attributed to Allah, the Glorified. There is no difference in this regard between what contains informative reports and what contains performative speech, nor between a long Surah and a short one, even down to a single verse. The accusation of fabrication can apply to the whole because it is a denial of the attribution pertaining to the whole.

Second: the verse does not challenge based on the eloquence (البلاغة) and fluency (الفصاحة) of the Quran alone. Rather, the context in this verse and other verses of challenge (التحدي) testifies that the challenge is based on the Quran's attribute of perfection (الكمال) and its quality of excellence (الفضيلة). This includes its containment of the core of Divine Gnosis (المعارف الإلهية), the comprehensive laws (جوامع الشرائع) consisting of devotional rulings (الأحكام العبادية), civil, political, economic, and judicial laws, noble ethics, refined manners, stories of the prophets and past nations, prophecies and reports of the Unseen (الأخبار الغيبية), descriptions of the angels, jinn, the heavens, and the earth, wisdom, exhortation, the promise and the threat, reports of the Beginning and the Return (البدء والعود), the strength of argument, the majesty of expression, and light and guidance—all without any part of it contradicting another. Add to this its reaching a level of eloquence and fluency that human hands fall short of attaining.

The researchers among the scholars of the early generations and those who followed them fell short when they restricted its inimitability (إعجاز) to its eloquence and fluency. They wrote books and authored treatises on this, which diverted them from contemplating its realities and deepening their understanding of its gnosis. It led them to consider the meanings as "matters cast upon the road," equal for the bedouin and the city-dweller, the commoner and the elite, the ignorant and the learned, and that the merit belongs to the composition of the word over the composition of the meaning, and that there is no value in what lies beyond that.

Yet Allah, the Exalted, has described it with every beautiful attribute relevant to the challenge, such as describing it as light (نور), mercy (رحمة), guidance (هدى), wisdom (حكمة), exhortation (موعظة), proof (برهان), a clarification for all things (تبيان لكل شيء), an elaboration of the Book (تفصيل الكتاب), a healing for the believers (شفاء للمؤمنين), a decisive word (قول فصل) that is not a jest, and that it is the "positions of the stars" (مواقع النجوم), and that there is no contradiction in it. He did not explicitly specify its eloquence alone.

He made the statement absolute: that they could not bring its like even if they called upon whomever they could besides Allah, and even if jinn and men gathered for that purpose and were supporters of one another—and He did not restrict the speech to eloquence and fluency.

We have detailed the discussion on the inimitability of the Quran in the commentary on His saying: وَإِنْ كُنْتُمْ فِي رَيْبٍ مِمَّا نَزَّلْنا عَلى عَبْدِنا فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِنْ مِثْلِهِ "And if you are in doubt concerning what We have sent down to Our servant, then bring a surah like it" (2:23) in the first volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِما لَمْ يُحِيطُوا بِعِلْمِهِ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِهِمْ تَأْوِيلُهُ "No, they deny that whose knowledge they could not encompass, and whose interpretation has not yet come to them" (10:39) to the end of the verse. The verse explains the reality behind their lack of faith and their claim that it is a fabrication: they denied that part of the Quran whose knowledge they did not encompass, or they denied the Quran itself, whose knowledge they did not encompass. For it contains true gnosis of the nature of objective realities (العلوم الواقعية) that their knowledge cannot contain, and its interpretation (التأويل) has not yet come to them—that is, the interpretation of that which they denied—such that it would compel them to believe in it.

This is what the context of the meaning requires. Thus, His saying: وَلَمَّا يَأْتِهِمْ تَأْوِيلُهُ "and whose interpretation has not yet come to them" refers to the Day of Resurrection, as supported by His saying, the Exalted: هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا تَأْوِيلَهُ يَوْمَ يَأْتِي تَأْوِيلُهُ يَقُولُ الَّذِينَ نَسُوهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ قَدْ جاءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنا بِالْحَقِّ فَهَلْ لَنا مِنْ شُفَعاءَ فَيَشْفَعُوا لَنا أَوْ نُرَدُّ فَنَعْمَلَ غَيْرَ الَّذِي كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ "Do they await anything but its interpretation? The day its interpretation comes, those who had forgotten it before will say, ‘Our Lord’s apostles certainly brought the truth. Are there any intercessors to intercede for us? Or can we be sent back, so that we may act differently from what we did?’" (7:53).

This supports what we previously established in the commentary on His saying: ابْتِغاءَ الْفِتْنَةِ وَابْتِغاءَ تَأْوِيلِهِ وَما يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُ إِلَّا اللهُ "seeking to cause unrest and seeking to interpret it. But no one knows its interpretation except Allah" (3:7) in the third volume of this book: that the meaning of interpretation (التأويل) — the ultimate reality and objective source upon which a statement or concept rests — in Quranic usage is the reality upon which a meaning depends, whether it be a ruling, a piece of gnosis, a story, or other objective realities, without it being of the nature of a [mental] meaning itself. Furthermore, the entire Quran and all it contains of gnosis, rulings, reports, or otherwise, has an interpretation (تأويل).

This is also supported by His subsequent saying: كَذلِكَ كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ "In the same way did those who were before them deny" (10:39). The comparison implies that those polytheists before them also denied what their prophets called them to because they did not encompass its knowledge and its interpretation had not yet come to them. Thus, the components of the religious call brought by the other prophets—gnosis and rulings—have an interpretation, just as the gnosis and rulings of the Quran have an interpretation, without it being of the nature of concepts and the meanings of words as they imagined.

The sum of the meaning is that these polytheists, who accuse the Quran of being a fabrication, are like the polytheists and disbelievers of previous nations. They were confronted by matters of the religious call, with its gnosis and rulings, which they did not encompass with knowledge such that they could be certain and believe. Their ignorance drove them to deny them, while the day has not yet come when their interpretation and the reality of their matter will be manifested to them—a manifestation that would compel them toward certainty and belief. That is the Day of Resurrection, in which the veil will be lifted from the face of realities in their objective existence. These people denied and wronged, just as those before them denied and wronged; so look at what was the end of those wrongdoers, so that you may surmise what will befall these people.

This is what a precise investigation into the meaning of the verse yields. The commentators have various differing opinions based on their views regarding the meaning of "interpretation" (التأويل), but there is no benefit in addressing them here, as we have exhaustively examined their views previously.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِهِ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِهِ وَرَبُّكَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُفْسِدِينَ "Some of them believe in it, and some of them do not believe in it, and your Lord knows best the agents of corruption." (10:40). He divided them into two groups: those who believe in the Quran and those who do not. Then He alluded to those who do not believe as being "agents of corruption" (مفسدون). From this, it is concluded that those who deny what is in the Quran only do so because they are agents of corruption.

Thus, the verse is to clarify the state they are in—the belief of some and the disbelief of others—and that disbelief arises from the vice of corruption (الإفساد).

As for what some have mentioned in the commentary of the verse—that the meaning is: "Your people will not be like those wrongdoers before them who denied their messengers except for a few, and whose end was the punishment of extirpation; rather, your people will be two groups: a group that will believe in this Quran and a group that will never believe"—this is a meaning entirely outside the indication of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ كَذَّبُوكَ فَقُلْ لِي عَمَلِي وَلَكُمْ عَمَلُكُمْ "If they deny you, say, ‘My deeds belong to me and your deeds belong to you’" (10:41) to the end of the verse. This is a formal instruction (تلقين) on disavowal (التبري) in the event of their denial of him. It is one of the stages of championing the truth by one who has risen to revive it. The way is to urge people toward it if they can be urged; otherwise, one must disavowal them so they do not hold him responsible for their falsehood.

And His saying: أَنْتُمْ بَرِيئُونَ مِمَّا أَعْمَلُ وَأَنَا بَرِيءٌ مِمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ "You are quit of what I do and I am quit of what you do" (10:41) is an explanation of His saying: لِي عَمَلِي وَلَكُمْ عَمَلُكُمْ "My deeds belong to me and your deeds belong to you."

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَسْتَمِعُونَ إِلَيْكَ أَفَأَنْتَ تُسْمِعُ الصُّمَّ وَلَوْ كانُوا لا يَعْقِلُونَ "Some of them listen to you. But can you make the deaf hear even if they do not understand?" (10:42). The interrogation is for negation (الإنكار). His saying: وَلَوْ كانُوا لا يَعْقِلُونَ "even if they do not understand" is a contextual clue that what is meant by the negation of hearing is the negation of what accompanies it—the understanding of what the spoken word indicates, which is called the "hearing of the heart."

The meaning is: Among them are those who listen to you while they are deaf, having no hearing in their hearts; and you are not capable of making them hear when they possess no hearing.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَنْظُرُ إِلَيْكَ "And some of them look at you" (10:43) to the end of the verse. The discussion regarding this is analogous to the discussion regarding the preceding verse.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَظْلِمُ النَّاسَ شَيْئاً وَلكِنَّ النَّاسَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ "Indeed Allah does not wrong people in the least; rather, it is people who wrong themselves." (10:44). This is intended to indicate that the deprivation of hearing and sight—in the form of deafness and blindness—afflicting these people is a result of their wronging themselves, without Allah, the Exalted, having wronged them by stripping them of hearing and sight. For they were only given what they brought upon themselves.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ كَأَنْ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا ساعَةً مِنَ النَّهارِ يَتَعارَفُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ "On the day He will gather them, [it will be] as if they had not stayed [in the world] but for an hour of the day, getting acquainted with one another." (10:45). The apparent meaning of the verse is that "the day" (يَوْمَ) is an adverbial phrase (ظرف) related to His saying: قَدْ خَسِرَ "certainly they are losers" etc. His saying: كَأَنْ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا ساعَةً "as if they had not stayed but for an hour" is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the plural pronoun in "He will gather them" (يَحْشُرُهُمْ). His saying: يَتَعارَفُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ "getting acquainted with one another" is a second circumstantial qualifier explaining the first.

The meaning is: Those who denied the encounter with Allah have certainly lost on the day He gathers them to Him, while they deem this worldly life insignificant, considering it like a stay of an hour of the day, and they recognize one another without any of them denying or forgetting the other.

Some have mentioned that His saying: كَأَنْ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا is an adjective for "day" or an adjective for the omitted verbal noun (المصدر) indicated by His saying: يَحْشُرُهُمْ. Others have mentioned that His saying: يَتَعارَفُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ is an adjective for "hour" (ساعة). These are remote possibilities not supported by the wording.

In any case, the verse contains a return to the discourse on the "encounter" (اللقاء) mentioned at the beginning of the Surah, and a shift back to what was mentioned just now: that it is expected that the interpretation (تأويل) of the religion will come to them.

It is as if it says: Even if the interpretation of the Quran has not yet come to them, they should not be deceived by remaining fixed upon the outward appearances of this worldly life, nor should they consider the duration long or the term slow. For they shall be gathered to Allah and will witness that the worldly life was nothing but a brief enjoyment, and the stay therein was but a short stay, as if they had not stayed but for an hour of the day, recognizing one another.

On that day, their loss in denying the encounter with Allah will become manifest to them through direct vision. This will occur through the arrival of the interpretation of the religion, the uncovering of the reality of the matter, the manifestation of the light of monotheism (التوحيد) as it truly is, and the clarity that the kingdom on that day belongs to Allah, the One, the All-Paramount (القهار), exalted is His state.

Ayahs

وَإِمَّا نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى نَعِدُهُمْ أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ فَإِلَيْنَا مَرْجِعُهُمْ ثُمَّ ٱللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ عَلَىٰ مَا يَفْعَلُونَ (٤٦)
Whether We show you a part of what We promise them, or take you away [before that], [in any case] their return will be to Us and Allah will be witness to what they do. (46)
وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَّسُولٌ ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِىَ بَيْنَهُم بِٱلْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٤٧)
There is an apostle for every nation; so when their apostle comes, judgement is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged. (47)
وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٤٨)
They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, should you be truthful?’ (48)
قُل لَّآ أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِى ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۗ لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ ۚ إِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهُمْ فَلَا يَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ سَاعَةً ۖ وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ (٤٩)
Say, ‘I have no control over any benefit for myself nor [over] any harm except what Allah may wish. There is a time for every nation: when their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it.’ (49)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَتَىٰكُمْ عَذَابُهُۥ بَيَـٰتًا أَوْ نَهَارًا مَّاذَا يَسْتَعْجِلُ مِنْهُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (٥٠)
Say, ‘Tell me, should His punishment overtake you by night or day, [you will not be able to avert it]; so what part of it do the guilty seek to hasten?’ (50)
أَثُمَّ إِذَا مَا وَقَعَ ءَامَنتُم بِهِۦٓ ۚ ءَآلْـَٔـٰنَ وَقَدْ كُنتُم بِهِۦ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ (٥١)
‘What! Do you believe it when it has befallen? Now? While you would seek to hasten it [earlier]?!’ (51)
ثُمَّ قِيلَ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلْخُلْدِ هَلْ تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْسِبُونَ (٥٢)
Then it will be said to those who were wrongdoers, ‘Taste the everlasting punishment. Shall you not be requited for what you used to earn?’ (52)
۞ وَيَسْتَنۢبِـُٔونَكَ أَحَقٌّ هُوَ ۖ قُلْ إِى وَرَبِّىٓ إِنَّهُۥ لَحَقٌّ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ (٥٣)
They inquire of you, ‘Is it true?’ Say, ‘Yes! By my Lord, it is true, and you cannot frustrate [Him].’ (53)
وَلَوْ أَنَّ لِكُلِّ نَفْسٍ ظَلَمَتْ مَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لَٱفْتَدَتْ بِهِۦ ۗ وَأَسَرُّوا۟ ٱلنَّدَامَةَ لَمَّا رَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ۖ وَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُم بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۚ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٥٤)
Were any soul that has done wrong to possess whatever there is on the earth, it would surely offer it for ransom. They will hide their remorse when they sight the punishment; and judgement will be made between them with justice and they will not be wronged. (54)
أَلَآ إِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٥٥)
Look! To Allah indeed belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. Look! Allah’s promise is indeed true; but most of them do not know. (55)
هُوَ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٥٦)
It is He who gives life and brings death, and to Him you shall be brought back. (56)

(Commentary)

The verses announce a recurring divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — the established way and pattern of Allah in dealing with His creation — which is that Allah, the Glorified, has decreed a true decree that can neither be averted nor altered: that He sends a messenger to every nation to convey His message to them, and then He judges between him and them with a decisive judgment by sending down punishment upon them, saving the believers, and destroying the deniers.

Then He commands the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to inform them that this nation will be subject to the same divine sunnah that occurred in past nations, without being excluded from its universality. However, in what Allah prompted him to answer regarding their question about the timing of the punishment, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) only mentioned that the decree is inevitable and that for every nation there is a term (أجل) — a fixed lifespan and appointed time — just like an individual, reaching the end of its life's duration. As for the specific time of its descent, it has been left entirely ambiguous.

We have previously mentioned regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا كَانَ اللَّهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَأَنْتَ فِيهِمْ وَمَا كَانَ اللَّهُ مُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ "But Allah would not punish them while you were among them, nor will Allah punish them while they plead for forgiveness" (8:33), that the verse is not devoid of an intimation that the blessing of seeking forgiveness (الاستغفار) will be snatched away from the nation after the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and then the punishment will descend upon them. It has also been previously established that the evidence points to that verse being Medinan; thus, coming after these Meccan verses, it serves as a general clarification after ambiguity and is among the prophecies (ملاحم) of the Quran.

Some commentators have interpreted the mention of punishment in these verses as the punishment of the Hereafter, but the context (سياق) of the verses rejects this.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِمَّا نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ الَّذِي نَعِدُهُمْ أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ فَإِلَيْنَا مَرْجِعُهُمْ ثُمَّ اللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ عَلَى مَا يَفْعَلُونَ "Whether We show you part of what We promise them, or take you away, to Us is their return, then Allah is witness to what they do" (10:46). The word imma nuriyannaka (إما نرينك) is originally in naraka (إن نرك), with the addition of ma (ما) and the heavy nun (النون الثقيلة) for emphasis. The alternation between "showing" and "taking away" (death) is for equalization and to encompass all possibilities. The meaning is: to Us is their return in any case. The word thumma (ثم) denotes a sequence in the order of speech rather than time. The verse is intended to comfort the heart of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and to serve as a preamble to the discourse on the decree of punishment which the subsequent verses will detail.

The meaning is: be at peace, for We shall surely bring upon them what We have promised them, whether We show you some of that or take your soul before showing it to you; for their affair is ultimately Ours, and We are witnesses to their deeds that necessitate punishment—they are neither hidden from Us nor forgotten by Us.

The transition (الالتفات) from His saying "We show you" (نُرِيَنَّكَ) to His saying "then Allah is witness" (ثُمَّ اللهُ شَهِيدٌ) is to indicate the cause of the judgment, for Allah, the Glorified, is a witness over every act by virtue of His divinity (ألوهيته).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذَا جَاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ "There is a messenger for every nation: so when their messenger comes, judgment is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged" (10:47). This is a divine decree that decomposes into two decrees: first, that every nation among nations has a messenger who carries Allah's message to them and conveys it; and second, that when he comes to them and conveys His message, and they differ—some believing him and others denying him—Allah judges and decrees between them with equity (القسط) and justice (العدل) without them being wronged. This is what the context of the speech provides.

From this, it becomes clear that His saying "so when their messenger comes" (فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ) contains a conciseness through omission (إيجاز بالحذف) and implication. The estimated meaning is: "so when their messenger comes to them and conveys the message, and his people differ by denial and belief..." This is evidenced by His saying: "judgment is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged," for judgment only occurs in matters where there is disagreement. Therefore, the question of equity and the absence of injustice in judgment is more immediate to the mind in the context of punishment and harm.

The difference between a messenger (الرسول) and a prophet (النبي) has already been discussed in the chapters on Prophethood (النبوة) in the second volume of this book. This judgment mentioned in the verse is among the specific characteristics of messengership (الرسالة) rather than prophethood.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَى هَذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صَادِقِينَ "They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’" (10:48). This is a question from them regarding the time of this promised judgment, which is the judgment between them in this world. The questioners are some of the polytheists (المشركين) contemporary to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The evidence for this is His command to answer them with His saying: "Say, ‘I have no control over any benefit or harm for myself...’" etc. Thus, the opinion of some that the question is about the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, or that the questioners were polytheists from previous nations, is not to be considered.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لَا أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِي ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ اللَّهُ لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ "Say, ‘I have no power to benefit or harm myself, except what Allah may wish. There is a term for every nation...’" (10:49) to the end of the verse. Since their saying "When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?" is in the sense of saying: "At what time will your Lord fulfill what He promised you, or bring what He threatened us with—that He will judge between us and you, destroying us and saving you and those who believe in you, so the atmosphere clears for you, the earth becomes yours, and you are rid of our evil? Why has He not hastened that for you?" This is because their speech is intended to express haste (استعجال) as a way of challenging and mocking, as indicated by the subsequent verses regarding their seeking to hasten. This is similar to their saying: لَوْ مَا تَأْتِينَا بِالْمَلَائِكَةِ إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ "Why do you not bring us the angels, if you are truthful?" (15:7).

The Glorified prompted the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to begin the answer by clarifying that he does not possess for himself any harm (ضراً) to repel it, nor any benefit (نفعاً) to attract it and hasten it, except what Allah wills for him to possess of harm or benefit. Thus, the entire matter belongs to Allah, the Glorified, and their suggestion to him to hasten the judgment and punishment is born of ignorance.

Then He answers their question regarding the actual specification of the time with a general response, by turning away from specifying the time and turning toward mentioning the necessity of its occurrence. As for the first part, it is from the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — which none knows but Allah, and His affair over which none has authority but Him. His saying has already appeared in the verses of this Surah: وَيَقُولُونَ لَوْلَا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ فَقُلْ إِنَّمَا الْغَيْبُ لِلَّهِ فَانْتَظِرُوا إِنِّي مَعَكُمْ مِنَ الْمُنْتَظِرِينَ "They say, ‘Why has not a sign been sent down to him from his Lord?’ Say, ‘[The knowledge of] the Unseen belongs only to Allah. So wait; I too am waiting along with you.’" (10:20).

As for the second part, namely the necessity of its occurrence, He explained this by pointing to a reality that is among the universal laws (النواميس العامة) operating in the cosmos, by which the knot is untied and the doubt is repelled. This reality is that every nation has a term (أجل) that does not bypass them, nor do they bypass it; it is coming to them inevitably. When it comes to them, it does not miss its mark or its hour. This is His saying: لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ إِذَا جَاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ فَلَا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ سَاعَةً وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ "There is a term for every nation: when their term expires, they shall not defer it by a single hour, nor shall they advance it" (10:49). That is: you are a nation among nations, so you inevitably have a term like theirs; when it comes to you, you shall not defer it by an hour nor advance it.

If they comprehend this speech and reflect upon it, it becomes clear to them that every nation has a social life (حياة اجتماعية) beyond the individual life (الحياة الفردية) of each of its members. Its life has a duration and lifespan decreed by Allah, and it has its share of felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة), obligation (التكليف), guidance (الرشد), error (الغي), reward, and punishment. It is something that divine governance (التدبير الإلهي) has cared for just as it cares for the individual human being, step by step.

They are guided to this by what history relates and what the ruins of their destroyed homes and empty dwellings express. The Quran has narrated to them the news of some of them, such as the people of Noah, the 'Ad (the people of Hud), the Thamud (the people of Salih), the Chaldeans (the people of Abraham), the people of Sodom and the other subverted cities (the people of Lot), the Copts (the people of Pharaoh), and others.

These were vanished nations whose bells fell silent and whose breaths were extinguished. They did not vanish except through punishment and destruction, and they were not punished except after their messengers came to them with clear proofs. No messenger came to a people among them but that they differed regarding the truth he brought; some believed in it, while others—the majority—denied it.

This indicates to them that this nation—having differed regarding the truth when it came to them—will have Allah judge between His Messenger and them. He will seize them as He seized the nations that passed before them, for indeed, Allah is ever on the watch.

The reflecting researcher must take note that although Allah, the Glorified, began His threat with the polytheists (المشركين), He threatened the criminals (المجرمين) during His speech, so the threat became attached to them. Among the people of the Qiblah are criminals just like others; let them then expect a lasting punishment (عذاباً واصباً) by which Allah will judge between them and His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Let them forget what Satan casts into their hearts—that this nation is a "mercy-granted nation" (أمة مرحومة) from whom Allah has lifted the punishment of this world out of honor for His Prophet, the Prophet of Mercy, and that they are safe from Allah's punishment even if they indulge in every sin and transgression and tear every veil. This is despite the fact that there is no honor with Allah except through godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah and guarding oneself against His displeasure. He has addressed the believers of this nation with the likes of His saying: لَيْسَ بِأَمَانِيِّكُمْ وَلَا أَمَانِيِّ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءًا يُجْزَ بِهِ "It is not by your fancies, nor by the fancies of the People of the Book: whoever commits evil shall be requited for it" (4:123).

Perhaps a transgressor might go further and append the punishment of the Hereafter to the punishment of this world, mentioning that the nation is forgiven, both its righteous and its sinful. Thus, nothing would remain for them in this world except the "honor" of being able to do whatever they wish, for Allah has draped over them the veil of safety; nor in the Hereafter except forgiveness and Paradise.

In such a case, nothing would remain of the religion (الملة) and the Sacred Law (الشريعة) except that they are arbitrary obligations and rulings played with by the Lord of the Worlds—"He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned." Exalted is He far above what they say.

All of this is from turning away from the remembrance of Allah and forsaking His Book: وَقَالَ الرَّسُولُ يَا رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِي اتَّخَذُوا هَذَا الْقُرْآنَ مَهْجُورًا "And the Apostle will say, ‘O my Lord! Indeed my people followed this Quran as a forsaken thing’" (25:30).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَتَاكُمْ عَذَابُهُ بَيَاتًا أَوْ نَهَارًا مَاذَا يَسْتَعْجِلُ مِنْهُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ "Say, ‘Tell me, if His punishment should overtake you by night or day, what part of it will the guilty hasten?’" (10:50) to the end of the two verses. Al-bayat (البيات) and al-tabyit (التبييت) mean coming by night, and it is mostly used for evil, such as an enemy attacking his enemy at night.

Since their saying "When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?" was in the sense of hastening the sign of punishment that would compel them to believe, He returned—after explaining the certainty of its occurrence—to rebuking and censuring them from two aspects. First, He rebuked them for hastening the punishment, which is a sudden punishment that prudence dictates one should be wary of, not hasten. So Allah said, prompting His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "Say, ‘Tell me’" (قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ) and inform me, "if His punishment should overtake you by night" (بَيَاتًا) or "by day" (نَهَارًا), for it is a punishment that will only come to you suddenly since you do not know the time of its descent; "what part of it" (مَاذَا) of the punishment "will the guilty hasten?" (يَسْتَعْجِلُ مِنْهُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ), meaning: what are you hastening of it while you are criminals whom it will not bypass when it comes?

In His saying "what part of it will the guilty hasten?" there is a transition (التفات) from direct address to the third person. The subtle point (النكتة) in this is the consideration of their state, so as not to confront them with explicit evil, and to point to the basis for the descent of punishment upon them, which is their criminality (إجرامهم).

Second, He rebuked them for delaying their faith until a time when faith will not benefit them, which is the time of the descent of punishment. For the sign of punishment certainly compels them to believe, as is experienced by the faith of a person when on the brink of destruction. On the other hand, faith is repentance (توبة), and repentance is not accepted upon the appearance of the sign of punishment and the imminence of death.

So Allah said: أَثُمَّ إِذَا مَا وَقَعَ "Is it then, when it has come to pass," the punishment, آمَنْتُمْ بِهِ "that you will believe in it?"—meaning in the Quran, the religion, or Allah. آلْآنَ "Now?"—meaning: do you believe in it at this moment and time? وَقَدْ كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ "While you were seeking to hasten it?" The meaning of their hastening was their lack of concern for this punishment and their belittling of it through mockery.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ قِيلَ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا ذُوقُوا عَذَابَ الْخُلْدِ هَلْ تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا بِمَا كُنْتُمْ تَكْسِبُونَ "Then it will be said to those who were unjust, ‘Taste the everlasting punishment! Are you requited except for what you used to earn?’" (10:52). It is most likely that this verse is connected to His saying: "There is a term for every nation..." etc. Thus, the first verse explains the certainty of the occurrence of punishment upon them and their destruction, and the second verse explains that it will be said to them after the occurrence and destruction: "Taste the everlasting punishment," which is the punishment of the Hereafter, and you are not requited except for your deeds that you used to earn and the sins you carry. The address is existential (تكويني), used as a metaphor for the punishment encompassing them and reaching them. According to this meaning, the two verses "Say, ‘Tell me...’" until His saying "...seeking to hasten it" are parenthetical (اعتراض).

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَسْتَنْبِئُونَكَ أَحَقٌّ هُوَ قُلْ إِي وَرَبِّي إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ وَمَا أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ "They ask you to inform them: ‘Is it true?’ Say, ‘Yes, by my Lord! It is indeed true, and you cannot frustrate [Him].’" (10:53). Yastanbi'unaka (يَسْتَنْبِئُونَكَ) means they ask you for news, and His saying "Is it true?" (أَحَقٌّ هُوَ) is an explanation of it. The pronoun, as provided by the context, refers to the judgment or the punishment; the outcome is the same. Allah, the Glorified, commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to emphasize the statement in affirming it from all sides; in other words, to answer them by affirming the existence of the cause (المقتضي) and the absence of any obstacle (المانع).

His saying: "Say, ‘Yes, by my Lord! It is indeed true’" is an affirmation of its occurrence, and He emphasized the speech with an oath, the nominal sentence (الجملة الاسمية), inna (إن), and the lam (اللام). His saying: "and you cannot frustrate [Him]" (وَمَا أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ) is a clarification that there is no obstacle there to prevent the punishment from befalling you.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّ لِكُلِّ نَفْسٍ ظَلَمَتْ مَا فِي الْأَرْضِ لَافْتَدَتْ بِهِ "Were every soul that has been unjust to possess whatever there is on the earth, it would surely offer it as ransom..." (10:54) to the end of the verse. This is an allusion to the severity of the punishment and the importance of escaping it in their view. "Concealing regret" (إسرار الندامة) means hiding and keeping it secret for fear of the enemies' gloating and the like. It appears that what is meant by judgment and punishment in the verse is the worldly judgment and punishment alone.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَا إِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَلَا إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ "Look! To Allah indeed belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. Look! Allah’s promise is indeed true, but most of them do not know" (10:55). This verse and the one following it are a demonstrative proof (بيان برهاني) for the truth of what He mentioned—that it is a reality occurring with no obstacle to prevent it. For if everything in the heavens and the earth is owned by Allah alone without any partner, then every conceivable disposal (تصرف) within them belongs to Him, the Exalted. No one else has any disposal except by His permission. Thus, when He disposes of something, it is attributed to His will alone, without being attributed to any other external cause (مقتض) that acts upon His Sacred Essence to compel Him to act, or being restricted by the absence of an external obstacle (مانع) which, if present, would act upon Him, the Glorified, by preventing Him from acting. He, the Exalted, does what He does from Himself without being linked to an external cause or an external obstacle. Therefore, when He, the Glorified, wills something, He does it without any helper or hinderer; and when He makes a promise, it is a truth that cannot be averted, without Him being turned away from His promise by any diverter.

Careful reflection on His absolute, real ownership (ملكه المطلق الحقيقي) leads to the knowledge that His promise is truth unmixed with falsehood. However, most of them—who are the common people—do not know, due to their inability to reflect on these essential inquiries or their fascination with simplistic understanding and their following the path of the masses.

Accordingly, they measure His ownership, the Exalted, against the ownership of the exalted and dominant among humans. They find one of their great ones having been given kingdom and authority and everything competed for; they see him as having absolute power, doing what he wishes and decreeing what he wants. Then they find him perhaps intending and striving, yet what he intended does not occur; or he makes a promise and then does not fulfill it out of consideration for his own interest or that of others, or due to a hindering obstacle. Thus, they measure the affair of Allah, the Exalted, against his affair, and His promise against his promise. Furthermore, a promise to them is a statement that by its nature may or may not correspond to external reality.

This is while the reality of the meaning of His ownership, authority, the vastness of His power, and the effectiveness of His will is that people believe that for Him and conceive Him as great among them; yet if the calamities of time were to crush him one day and destroy him, or if the beliefs of people were to change regarding him for any reason, he would be stripped of the kingdom and power he possessed. The meaning of the occurrence of what he willed or loved is that cosmic causes (الأسباب الكونية) assisted him in that and agreed with what he loved. If the totality of causes did not assist or agree with him, he would not be able to compel them to submit to the power he imagines for himself, just as they do not agree with him regarding matters like death, life, youth, old age, health, sickness, and many other things. Thus, he has no part in the command.

But He, the Glorified, is the Owner (مالك) of His creation in the sense that the existence of everything is sustained by Him, coming into being and transforming by His command, and contingent upon His permission. Whatever He disposes of, He only disposes from Himself, not from the requirement of an external cause influencing Him or the absence of an obstacle hindering Him from His act. Thus, nothing is attributed except to Allah, the Exalted, Himself, or to another by His permission to the extent He has permitted. How then can anything fail to comply with His will such that it returns to another, when there is no "other" there to return to or be attributed to?

His saying, the Exalted, is His act, which by itself indicates His intent. How then can falsehood (الكذب) creep into it when it is the very fabric of external reality, and external reality (العين الخارجي) contains no falsehood? Falsehood and error are only the lot of mental concepts (المفاهيم الذهنية) in terms of their correspondence to the external world. How can His promise be void when His promise to us is His act that is hidden from our view and future to us, while He has directed the totality of causes toward it and it cannot be averted?

Careful reflection on these realities illuminates for the reflecting researcher the meaning of His ownership, the Exalted, of what is in the heavens and the earth, and that the necessary consequence of this is that Allah's promise is true, and that doubt regarding it only stems from ignorance of His station, the Exalted.

For this reason, He said first: "Look! To Allah indeed belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth," then followed it with His saying as a conclusion from it: "Look! Allah’s promise is indeed true," then He added a qualification, saying: "but most of them do not know," then He explained His ownership by His saying: "He gives life and brings death..." etc., in the following verse.

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ "He gives life and brings death, and to Him you will be brought back" (10:56). This is an argument for what preceded in the previous verse regarding His ownership, the Exalted, in relation to the human species. It is as if He, the Exalted, is saying: your entire affair—of life, death, and return—is to Allah, the Exalted; how then can you not be His property?

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, and in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَتَاكُمْ عَذَابُهُ بَيَاتًا — "meaning by night" — أَوْ نَهَارًا مَاذَا يَسْتَعْجِلُ مِنْهُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ — "This is a punishment that will descend at the end of time upon the transgressors among the people of the Qiblah—while they deny that punishment will descend upon them."

I say: The narration is supported by the verses and supports the commentary we have previously provided.

In the same source, with its chain of transmission from al-Hasan ibn Musa al-Khashshab, from a man, from Hammad ibn 'Isa, from one who narrated it, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: He was asked about His saying, Blessed and Exalted is He: وَأَسَرُّوا النَّدَامَةَ لَمَّا رَأَوُا الْعَذَابَ — "And they will conceal regret when they see the punishment." He said: It was said to him, "What benefit will concealing regret be to them while they are in the punishment?" He replied: "They disliked the gloating of the enemies."

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ قَدْ جَآءَتْكُم مَّوْعِظَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَشِفَآءٌ لِّمَا فِى ٱلصُّدُورِ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٥٧)
O mankind! There has certainly come to you an advice from your Lord, and a cure for what is in the breasts, and a guidance and mercy for the faithful. (57)
قُلْ بِفَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ وَبِرَحْمَتِهِۦ فَبِذَٰلِكَ فَلْيَفْرَحُوا۟ هُوَ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ (٥٨)
Say, ‘In Allah’s grace and His mercy—let them rejoice in that! It is better than what they amass.’ (58)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُم مَّآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُم مِّن رِّزْقٍ فَجَعَلْتُم مِّنْهُ حَرَامًا وَحَلَـٰلًا قُلْ ءَآللَّهُ أَذِنَ لَكُمْ ۖ أَمْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ تَفْتَرُونَ (٥٩)
Say, ‘Have you regarded what Allah has sent down for you of [His] provision, whereupon you have made some of it unlawful and [some] lawful?’ Say, ‘Did Allah give you the sanction [to do so], or do you fabricate a lie against Allah?’ (59)
وَمَا ظَنُّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٦٠)
What is the idea of those who fabricate lies against Allah [concerning their situation] on the Day of Resurrection? Indeed Allah is gracious to mankind, but most of them do not give thanks. (60)
وَمَا تَكُونُ فِى شَأْنٍ وَمَا تَتْلُوا۟ مِنْهُ مِن قُرْءَانٍ وَلَا تَعْمَلُونَ مِنْ عَمَلٍ إِلَّا كُنَّا عَلَيْكُمْ شُهُودًا إِذْ تُفِيضُونَ فِيهِ ۚ وَمَا يَعْزُبُ عَن رَّبِّكَ مِن مِّثْقَالِ ذَرَّةٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَلَآ أَصْغَرَ مِن ذَٰلِكَ وَلَآ أَكْبَرَ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ (٦١)
You do not engage in any work, neither do you recite any part of the Quran, nor do you perform any deed without Our being witness over you when you are engaged therein. Not an atom’s weight escapes your Lord in the earth or in the sky, nor [is there] anything smaller than that nor bigger, but it is in a manifest Book. (61)
أَلَآ إِنَّ أَوْلِيَآءَ ٱللَّهِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٦٢)
Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve (62)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ يَتَّقُونَ (٦٣)
—those who have faith and are Godwary. (63)
لَهُمُ ٱلْبُشْرَىٰ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِكَلِمَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٦٤)
For them is good news in the life of this world and in the Hereafter. (There is no altering the words of Allah.) That is the great success. (64)
وَلَا يَحْزُنكَ قَوْلُهُمْ ۘ إِنَّ ٱلْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا ۚ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٦٥)
Do not grieve at their remarks; indeed all might belongs to Allah; He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (65)
أَلَآ إِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَمَا يَتَّبِعُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ شُرَكَآءَ ۚ إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ وَإِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ (٦٦)
Look! To Allah indeed belongs whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth. What do they pursue who invoke partners besides Allah? They merely follow conjectures and they just make surmises. (66)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ لِتَسْكُنُوا۟ فِيهِ وَٱلنَّهَارَ مُبْصِرًا ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ (٦٧)
It is He who made the night for you, that you may rest in it, and the day to provide visibility. There are indeed signs in that for people who listen. (67)
قَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَدًا ۗ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ ۖ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ۖ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ إِنْ عِندَكُم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍۭ بِهَـٰذَآ ۚ أَتَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٦٨)
They say, ‘Allah has taken a son!’ Immaculate is He! He is the All-sufficient. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth. You have no authority for this [statement]. Do you attribute to Allah what you do not know? (68)
قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ لَا يُفْلِحُونَ (٦٩)
Say, ‘Indeed those who fabricate lies against Allah will not be felicitous.’ (69)
مَتَـٰعٌ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ثُمَّ إِلَيْنَا مَرْجِعُهُمْ ثُمَّ نُذِيقُهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلشَّدِيدَ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ (٧٠)
An enjoyment in this world; then to Us shall be their return, then We shall make them taste the severe punishment because of what they used to defy. (70)

(Commentary)

The discourse in these verses returns to describing the Noble Quran with its noble attributes, followed by miscellaneous points connected to the previous discourse regarding the purpose of the Surah. Within them are exhortation, wisdom, and proof for various objectives, as well as a description of the friends of Allah (أولياء الله) and glad tidings for them.

His saying, the Exalted: «يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ قَدْ جَاءَتْكُم مَّوْعِظَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ» — O mankind! There has certainly come to you an advice from your Lord to the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "Exhortation (الوعظ) is a deterrent coupled with intimidation." Al-Khalil said: "It is a reminder of good in a way that softens the heart." Al-Idhah (العظة) and al-Maw'izah (الموعظة) are the nouns. (End of quote). The chest (الصدر) is well-known; since people found the heart within the chest, and they perceived that a human only perceives what he perceives through his heart—by which he understands matters, loves, hates, intends, dislikes, yearns, hopes, and wishes—they considered the chest to be the treasury for the secrets of the heart and the spiritual attributes (الصفات الروحية) within the human interior, including virtues and vices. Within virtues lie the health and uprightness of the heart, and within vices lie its sickness and disease. Vice is a malady (داء); it is said: "I healed my chest with such-and-such" when that which was in the chest of constriction and distress has departed. It is also said: "I healed my heart." Thus, the healing of the chests (شفاء الصدور) and the healing of what is in the chests is an allusion to the departure of the base spiritual attributes that bring misery to the human, disturb his happy life, and deprive him of the good of this world and the Hereafter.

Guidance (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — is the pointing toward the sought objective with gentleness, as mentioned by al-Raghib. A discussion on this has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: «فَمَن يُرِدِ اللَّهُ أَن يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلَامِ» — Whomever Allah desires to guide, He opens his breast to Islam (Al-An'am: 125) in the seventh volume of this book.

Mercy (الرحمة) is a specific affection in the heart arising from witnessing harm or deficiency in another, which impels the merciful one to mend the fracture and complete the deficiency. When attributed to Him, the Exalted, it carries the meaning of the result (النتيجة) rather than the origin of the affection, due to His transcendence above such things. Thus, it applies to His absolute bestowal and His emanation of existence (إفاضة الوجود) upon His creation.
