قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ قَدْ جَآءَكُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ فَمَنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۖ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ عَلَيْكُم بِوَكِيلٍ (١٠٨)
Say, ‘O mankind! The truth has already come to you from your Lord. Whoever is guided, is guided only for [the good of] his own soul, and whoever goes astray, goes astray only to its detriment, and it is not my business to watch over you.’ (108)
وَٱتَّبِعْ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ وَٱصْبِرْ حَتَّىٰ يَحْكُمَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ (١٠٩)
And follow that which is revealed to you, and be patient until Allah issues [His] judgement, and He is the best of judges. (109)

(Commentary)

These verses, the conclusion of the Surah, summarize its primary discourses by referring generally to monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts, the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and final gathering of souls for judgment, and prophethood (النبوة). They command the following of the Quran and patience while awaiting the judgment of Allah between the Prophet and his nation.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فِي شَكٍّ مِنْ دِينِي — "Say, ‘O mankind! If you are in doubt about my religion..." (10:104) etc. It has been mentioned more than once that religion (الدين) — the systematic way of life followed to attain true felicity — carries the meaning of obedience, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَأَخْلَصُوا دِينَهُمْ لِلَّهِ — "...and dedicate their religion purely to Allah" (4:146). It is also sometimes used to mean recompense (الجزاء).

His saying: إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فِي شَكٍّ مِنْ دِينِي — "If you are in doubt about my religion," means: in the path I tread and upon which I remain firm. A person’s doubt regarding the religion of another and their practiced path usually concerns their steadfastness upon it: will they remain settled and upright upon it? The polytheists used to covet the religion of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم and sometimes hoped they could turn him away from it, thereby escaping his call to monotheism (التوحيد) and his rejection of associating partners with the gods.

Thus, the meaning is: If you doubt that which I profess and to which I call—whether I will remain upright upon it—or if you doubt what my religion actually is and have not grasped the principle upon which it is built, then I openly declare its reality to you.

I clarify it for you: it is that I do not worship your gods, but I worship Allah alone.

In His saying: وَلكِنْ أَعْبُدُ اللهَ الَّذِي يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ — "...but I worship Allah, who causes you to die," He chose the attribute of "causing them to die" (توفيهم) rather than His other attributes because they only worshipped a deity due to their perceived need for it to repel harm or bring benefit. Death is a matter they do not doubt will strike them and that it belongs to Allah alone; thus, the pressing need for security from its harm necessitates the worship of Allah, the Glorified.

Furthermore, the choice of mentioning death (التوفي) serves as an allusion to threatening them. The preceding verses promised them punishment with a definitive promise, and the death of the polytheists is the appointed time for their punishment. This is supported by following His saying: وَلكِنْ أَعْبُدُ اللهَ الَّذِي يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ — "...but I worship Allah, who causes you to die," with His saying: أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "...and I have been commanded to be among the believers." Indeed, their salvation from punishment is part of the promise Allah mentioned in the two verses prior to this one: فَهَلْ يَنْتَظِرُونَ إِلَّا مِثْلَ أَيَّامِ الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ — "Do they await anything but the like of the days of those who passed away before them?" (10:102) until His saying: نُنْجِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "...[so do We] deliver the believers" (10:103).

The meaning is: Know and be certain that I do not worship your gods, but I worship Allah, who has promised punishment for the deniers among you and salvation for the believers, and He has commanded me to be among them just as He commanded me to avoid the worship of [false] gods.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْ أَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً — "And: ‘Set your face toward the religion with uprightness...’" (10:105) is coordinated with the position of His saying: وَأُمِرْتُ أَنْ — "and I have been commanded to...", for it carries the meaning of "and be among the believers." The discussion regarding the meaning of "setting one's face toward the upright religion" (إقامة الوجه للدين الحنيف) has passed more than once.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَدْعُ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ ما لا يَنْفَعُكَ وَلا يَضُرُّكَ — "And do not invoke besides Allah what neither profits you nor harms you..." (10:106) is a prohibition following a prohibition against polytheism (الشرك), and a clarification that polytheism places a person among the ranks of the wrongdoers (الظالمين), thereby making them deserving of what Allah has promised the wrongdoers in His speech.

Among the subtleties of expression is His saying when mentioning invocation (الدعاء): ما لا يَنْفَعُكَ وَلا يَضُرُّكَ — "what (ما) neither profits you nor harms you," whereas when mentioning worship (العبادة) He said: الَّذِينَ تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ — "those whom (الذين) you worship besides Allah" (10:104). This is because worship naturally ascribes consciousness and intellect to the object of worship; thus, it was appropriate to refer to them using الَّذِينَ (those who), which is used for beings possessed of knowledge and intellect. Although invocation (الدعاء) is similar because it is synonymous with worship, when He described the one invoked as that which neither profits nor harms—and it might be imagined that those with knowledge and intellect are capable of profiting or harming—He used the word ما (what/that which) to allude to the fact that they are inanimate objects (جماد) for whom the will to profit or harm cannot even be imagined.

In the expression itself—namely His saying: ما لا يَنْفَعُكَ وَلا يَضُرُّكَ — "what neither profits you nor harms you"—lies the proof for the prohibition of invoking them.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ يَمْسَسْكَ اللهُ بِضُرٍّ فَلا كاشِفَ لَهُ إِلَّا هُوَ — "Should Allah visit you with some distress, there is no one to remove it except Him..." (10:107) etc. This sentence is circumstantial (حالية) and serves as the completion of the clarification in the previous verse. The meaning is: Do not invoke besides Allah that from which you have no profit or harm, while the reality is that any distress Allah touches you with cannot be removed by any other, and any good He intends for you cannot be turned back by any other. He is the Subduer (القاهر) above all others; He visits His servants with good by His wish and will, and He is, nonetheless, All-Forgiving, All-Merciful, forgiving the sins of His servants and showing them mercy. His being characterized by these noble attributes, while others are completely devoid of them, necessitates the restriction of worship (العبادة) and invocation (الدعوة) to Him alone.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ قَدْ جاءَكُمُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ — "Say, ‘O mankind! The truth has already come to you from your Lord...’" (10:108), which is the Quran or the true call it contains. His saying: فَمَنِ اهْتَدى — "so whoever is guided..." to the end of the verse, is an announcement to them that they are endowed with free will in what they choose for themselves, without their choice being stripped away. This is explained through a reality: that since the Truth has come to them, its law is that whoever is guided to it is only guided for his own benefit, and whoever goes astray from it only goes astray to his own detriment. Thus, they may choose for themselves whatever they love of profit or harm. He صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم is not a trustee (وكيل) over them who undertakes on their behalf the actions that belong to them. Thus, the verse is an allusion to the necessity of their being guided to the Truth because their benefit lies within it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّبِعْ ما يُوحى إِلَيْكَ وَاصْبِرْ حَتَّى يَحْكُمَ اللهُ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ الْحاكِمِينَ — "And follow what is revealed to you, and be patient until Allah judges, and He is the best of judges" (10:109). This is a command to follow what is revealed to him and to be patient with the calamities and trials that befall him in the course of this following. It is a promise that Allah, the Glorified, will judge between him and the people, and He does not judge except with that which will be the delight of his eye. Thus, the verse includes a command for him to remain steadfast in the call, a consolation for what afflicts him, and a promise that the ultimate good end belongs to him.

The verse concludes with His judgment, the Exalted, which is the foundation upon which most of the verses of this Surah rely in their exposition. And Allah knows best.

بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم
